My annual letter: Relevance and reinvention
Agree & Join LinkedIn
Below is my annual letter, published today in our Annual Report 2024.
Dear shareholders, colleagues, customers, and partners:
Fiscal year 2024 was a pivotal year for Microsoft. We entered our 50th year as a company and the second year of the AI platform shift. With these milestones, I’ve found myself reflecting on how Microsoft has remained a consequential company decade after decade in an industry with no franchise value. And I realize that it’s because—time and time again, when tech paradigms have shifted—we have seized the opportunity to reinvent ourselves to stay relevant to our customers, our partners, and our employees. And that’s what we are doing again today.
Microsoft has been a platform and tools company from the start. We were founded in 1975 with a belief in creating technology that would enable others to create their own. And, nearly 50 years later, this belief remains at the heart of our mission to empower every person and every organization on the planet to achieve more.
This year, we moved from talking about AI to helping our customers translate it into real outcomes—one person, one organization, one institution, and one country at a time. We have made remarkable progress on this front across every industry. For example:
· Coles is generating 1.6 billion daily AI predictions across 850 Australian stores, ensuring every shopper finds what they need.
· Unilever is performing thousands of simulations with AI in the time it would take to run tens of laboratory experiments, as it accelerates its product development.
· Developers at Itaú, Brazil’s largest private bank, are coding more efficiently using our AI pair programmer, GitHub Copilot.
· Khan Academy is making tutoring more accessible for students and helping teachers plan more creative lessons, using our small language model Phi.
· Aquafarmers in Indonesia are improving their yields, thanks to an app built with the Azure OpenAI Service, as well as Azure IoT.
· In Kenya, street vendors now have access to credit for the first time, thanks to M-Kopa, a social enterprise using Azure ML to do its forecasting.
· And enterprise customers and their employees around the world, from Amgen and Disney, to Finastra and Vodafone, are using Microsoft 365 Copilot to become more creative and productive.
Financially, the year was also marked by record performance. We delivered over $245 billion in annual revenue, up 16 percent year-over-year, and over $109 billion in operating income, up 24 percent.
Going forward, we are focused on three priorities: First, prioritizing fundamentals, with security above all else. We launched the Secure Future Initiative (SFI) this year, bringing together every part of our organization to advance cybersecurity protection. Second, driving trustworthy AI innovation across our entire portfolio while continuing to scale our cloud business. And, finally, managing our cost structure dynamically to generate durable, long-term operating leverage. All three priorities are critical to our ability to continue thriving as a company as we raise the bar on our operational excellence, with a focus on continuous improvement across everything we do.
AGE OF AI
If we go back 70 years to the beginning of modern computing, our industry has had two dreams: First, can computers understand us instead of us having to understand computers? And second, as we digitize more of the world—including people, places, and things—can computers help us reason, plan, and act more effectively using all that information? Over the past year, we have had breakthroughs on both fronts.
The core underlying force behind these breakthroughs is scaling laws. Just like Moore’s Law drove the information revolution, the scaling laws of deep neural networks (DNN) and transformers are driving today’s AI revolution. Up until the DNN inflection point, progress in compute was keeping up with Moore’s Law—doubling every two years. But we have now started to see progress in AI performance double roughly every six months.
There are three capabilities coming together because of these scaling laws. First, we have a new natural user interface that is multimodal. It supports speech, images, and videos—both as input and output. We have memory that retains important context, recalling both our personal knowledge and data across devices, apps, and the web. And, finally, we have new reasoning and planning capabilities that help us understand complex context, complete end-to-end tasks on our behalf, and reduce our cognitive load.
This new world is being defined by a rich tapestry of AI agents, which can take action on our behalf, including personal agents across work and life, business process agents, and cross-organizational ones. These agents will be able to work in concert as a new input to help make small businesses more productive, make multinationals more competitive, make the public sector more efficient, and improve health and education outcomes broadly.
Microsoft has built three leading platforms to help our customers maximize their opportunity in this emerging agentic era: Copilot, which you can think of as the new UI for AI—the human interface for this agentic world; the Copilot stack, which brings together infrastructure, data, and app services to help customers build their own copilots and agents for their own business processes; and a new category of Copilot devices that are purpose-built for this new era, including the Copilot+ PCs we introduced this year.
OUR OPPORTUNITY
The innovation we have driven over the past year matters only if we translate it into enduring value for our customers. That’s why, across our tech stack, we are focused on helping people and organizations realize the benefits of AI.
Infrastructure
This year, we expanded our cloud and AI capacity, announcing new investments across five continents. These are long-term assets to drive new growth for the next decade and beyond, and ensure communities around the world have access to the compute they need to drive economic growth in this new era.
Our cloud now also offers top performance for AI training and inference and the most diverse selection of AI accelerators, including the latest from AMD and NVIDIA, as well as our own first-party silicon, Azure Maia, which we introduced last November.
More broadly, we continued to see sustained revenue growth from migrations as customers turn to Azure. Azure Arc is helping customers streamline their transition, as they secure, develop, and operate workloads with Azure services anywhere. We have 36,000 Arc customers, up 90 percent year-over-year. And we remain the hyperscale cloud of choice for SAP and Oracle workloads.
Data & AI
AI models are now key building blocks for every application. And with Azure AI, we are building out the app server for the AI age, providing access to the most diverse selection of models to meet customers’ unique cost, latency, and design considerations. We offer leading frontier models, thanks to our strategic partnership with OpenAI. With Phi-3, which we announced in April, we offer a family of powerful, small language models. And, with Models as a service, we provide API access to third-party models, including the latest from Cohere, Meta, and Mistral. In total, we have over 60,000 Azure AI customers, up nearly 60 percent year-over-year. This year, we also announced a partnership with G42, which will run its AI applications and services on our cloud, as we collaborate to bring our latest AI technologies to the United Arab Emirates and other countries.
AI does not get created without data. At the data layer, we are fundamentally rethinking what it means to be an analytics database or an operational data store in the world of AI. Our Microsoft Intelligent Data Platform provides customers with the broadest capabilities spanning databases, analytics, business intelligence, and governance—along with seamless integration with all our AI services. And Microsoft Fabric, our AI-powered, next-generation data platform we made generally available this year, now has over 14,000 paid customers who can go from data, to insights, to action—all within the same unified SaaS solution.
Digital & app innovation
From GitHub to Visual Studio, we have the most comprehensive developer tools. GitHub Copilot had a breakout year, as it became standard issue for developers in every industry. We now have more than 1.8 million paid subscribers and over 77,000 enterprise customers, up 180 percent year-over-year. They are realizing productivity gains of up to 55 percent while staying in their flow and bringing the joy back to coding. This year, we also introduced Copilot Workspace, a Copilot-native developer environment, which helps any developer go from idea, to code, to software—all in natural language.
We are also integrating generative AI across Power Platform, enabling anyone to use natural language to create apps, automate workflows, or build a website. In total, we now have 48 million monthly active users of Power Platform, up 40 percent year-over-year.
Modern work
Microsoft 365 Copilot is becoming a daily habit for knowledge workers, transforming their work, workflow, and work artifacts. Adoption has been faster than any other new Microsoft 365 suite. And employees at nearly 60 percent of the Fortune 500 now use Copilot to complete tasks faster, hold more effective meetings, and automate business workflows and processes. In fact, internal and external studies show as much as a 70 percent improvement in productivity using generative AI for specific work tasks. And early Microsoft 365 Copilot users were 29 percent faster in a series of general tasks like searching, writing, and summarizing.
And we’re going further, bringing the Web plus Work plus Pages together as the new AI design system for knowledge work. With Pages, which we just announced last month, you can take any information from the web or your work and turn it into a multiplayer, AI-powered canvas. You can ideate with AI and then easily share what you create for collaboration with other people.
And with Copilot Studio, customers can extend Copilot with agents and build their own agents that proactively respond to data and events from their own first- and third-party business data. To date, 50,000 organizations have used it. And, just this week, we announced new capabilities that will make it possible for customers to build autonomous agents using Copilot Studio.
Microsoft Teams remains essential to how hundreds of millions of people meet, call, chat, collaborate, and do business. This year, we rolled out to all customers a new version that is up to two times faster while using 50 percent less memory. And Teams Premium surpassed 3 million seats, up nearly 400 percent year-over-year, as organizations chose it for advanced features like end-to-end encryption and real-time translation.
Business applications
We’re using this AI moment to redefine our role in business applications, too. Dynamics 365 once again took share, as organizations use our AI-powered apps to transform their marketing, sales, service, finance, and supply chain functions.
And we are expanding our total addressable market by integrating Copilot into third-party systems as well. Our new Dynamics 365 Contact Center infuses generative AI throughout the contact center workflow in a customer’s existing CRM.
We are also extending Copilot to specific industries, including healthcare. With DAX Copilot, more than 400 healthcare organizations are increasing physician productivity and reducing burnout. On average, clinicians save more than five minutes per patient encounter. And 77 percent say it also improves documentation quality.
Security
As I mentioned earlier, security underpins every layer of our tech stack. We are doubling down on our Secure Future Initiative, as we implement our principles of secure by design, secure by default, and secure operations. And we are focused on making continuous progress across the six pillars of the initiative: protect tenants and isolate production systems; protect identities and secrets; protect networks; protect engineering systems; monitor and detect threats; and accelerate response and remediation. As part of this commitment, all Microsoft employees now have security as a “core priority,” holding each one of us accountable for building secure products and services.
We are continuously applying what we are learning and translating it into security innovation for our customers. A great example is Copilot for Security, which we made generally available this year. It brings together LLMs with domain-specific skills informed by our threat intelligence and 78 trillion daily security signals to provide security teams with actionable insights.
Devices & creativity
This year, we introduced an entirely new category of Windows PCs engineered to unleash the power of distributed AI across the cloud and edge. Copilot+ PCs are the fastest, most AI-ready Windows PCs ever built. They include a new system architecture designed to deliver best-in-class performance and breakthrough AI experiences. And we are working across our entire ecosystem to bring these to life, including with AMD, Intel, and Qualcomm, along with our OEM partners.
Professional social network
LinkedIn continues to see accelerated member growth and record engagement. We surpassed 1 billion members for the first time this year, as we combine our unique data with this new generation of AI to transform how people learn, sell, and get hired. LinkedIn Marketing Solutions continues to be a leader in B2B digital advertising, helping companies deliver the right message, to the right audience, on a safe and trusted platform. And when it comes to our subscription businesses, Premium signups increased 51 percent, and we are adding even more value to our members and customers with new AI tools and skilling opportunities.
Search, ads, and news
With Copilot, we’re taking the first steps toward creating an AI companion, one that’s always by your side, helping you feel smarter and more supported through natural conversations. The refreshed Copilot app we introduced earlier this month delivers a more intuitive design with more digestible, speedy, and fluent answers. It now adapts to you with a warm tone and a distinct style, providing not only information but encouragement, feedback, and advice as you navigate life’s everyday challenges—no matter how big or small. And we’re adding advanced capabilities like Voice and Vision that make it both more useful and more natural.
We also continue to apply generative AI to pioneer new approaches to how people search and browse. Microsoft Bing and Edge both took share again this year. And we introduced Copilot Pro, providing access to the latest models for quick answers and higher-quality image creation, and access to Copilot for Microsoft 365 Personal and Family subscribers.
Thousands of news and entertainment publishers trust us to reach new audiences with Microsoft Start. And we are also helping advertisers increase their ROI. Copilot in Microsoft Ad Platform helps marketers create campaigns and troubleshoot using natural language.
Gaming
We are bringing great games to more people on more devices. With our acquisition of Activision Blizzard King, which closed October 2023, we’ve added hundreds of millions of players to our ecosystem. We now have 20 franchises that have generated over $1 billion in lifetime revenue—from Candy Crush, Diablo, and Halo, to Warcraft, Elder Scrolls, and Gears of War. And with Xbox cloud gaming, we continue to innovate to offer players more ways to experience the games they love—where, when, and how they want. Finally, we brought four of our fan-favorite titles to Nintendo Switch and Sony PlayStation for the first time, as we continue to extend our content to new platforms.
OUR MISSION
Although we have made outstanding progress over the past year, we cannot take our permission to innovate—let alone operate—for granted. It is something we must earn.
We always say Microsoft will do well only if the world around us does well. And that’s why we are focused on four enduring commitments. They keep us grounded, serving as a guide as we make decisions, pushing us to ask critical questions to ensure the technology we create benefits everyone on the planet, as well as the planet itself.
How can we expand opportunity?
We are democratizing access to AI and the skills needed to harness its potential, ensuring that every person, organization, and community can benefit from the opportunities AI brings.
This year, we partnered with 375,000 nonprofits globally, providing $4.7 billion in donated and discounted technology—to help them build capacity, drive efficiencies, innovate, and increase their impact as they take on the world’s greatest challenges. Together with our partners, we also made significant strides in skilling. We have trained and certified 14.1 million people across 202 countries in digital skills as of June 2024. Over 80 percent of the learners were from groups furthest from opportunity in the digital economy.
And our work doesn’t stop there. We must do more to accelerate the equitable adoption of AI and narrow the digital divide. That’s why we’ve launched new AI skilling initiatives in partnership with governments, educational institutions, industry, and civil society—to help millions of people learn to use generative AI, develop AI tools, and lead AI adoption. This includes people from underserved communities, with a focus on young people, women, rural communities, and the Global South, as well as employees of social impact organizations.
How can we earn trust?
We recognize that trust is earned, not given. And we remain committed to earning trust every day, spanning cybersecurity, trustworthy AI, privacy, and digital safety.
Our Secure Future Initiative advances how we design, build, test, and operate our technology to ensure we deliver solutions that meet the highest possible standard of security. Our first SFI Progress Report highlights updates spanning culture, governance, technology, and operations, but we recognize that our work on security is never complete. We must and will do more. Our promise is to continually learn, improve, and adapt to the evolving needs of an increasingly complex security landscape.
We are focused on building AI that is trustworthy, meaning that it is secure, safe, and private. Our responsible AI practices, grounded in our foundational AI principles, help ensure we do this from the beginning. And we’re building on this commitment by introducing new product capabilities across our tech stack, ensuring that both our customers and developers are safeguarded at every level. Ultimately, these commitments and capabilities are key to fulfilling our mission. The world is looking to us to help defend and protect them, and we take that responsibility seriously. In May, we published our first AI Transparency Report, which outlines how we build generative applications responsibly and support our customers. We also released Goals and Governance: Goals and Lessons for AI, which draws lessons from other globally governed technologies like civil aviation and nuclear power. Through our Accelerating Foundation Models Research program, we’ve made grants to hundreds of projects in AI safety and alignment research, AI-driven scientific discovery, and beneficial applications of AI. And we launched our Global Perspectives Responsible AI Fellowship program, designed to center the voices of AI experts from the Global South and enable us to better understand AI’s impact on developing countries. As we drive AI innovation, we continue to respond to a changing privacy landscape. We provide tools to help our customers protect their privacy and control their data, and we have published several resources outlining our approach to privacy and AI for our consumer, commercial, and public sector customers.
How can we protect fundamental rights?
We are committed to protecting fundamental rights—extending the benefits of technology while mitigating its potential harms. For us, this means promoting responsible business practices, expanding connectivity and accessibility, protecting democracy, and advancing a fair and inclusive society.
Over the past year, as regulators increasingly required greater transparency and process consistency across corporate supply chains and human rights efforts, we ensured compliance with reporting and due diligence directives. Going forward, we’ll continue to respect global human rights and laws and take steps to mitigate the impact of our operations and our technology on the people in our value chain.
As we build and deploy more AI solutions, connectivity and accessibility are foundational. Since 2017, we’ve extended access to affordable high-speed internet to over 100 million people, including nearly 40 million in Africa. And we remain focused on building inclusive, accessible AI that empowers people across the spectrum of disability.
More than 4 billion people will vote this year in their respective elections, making it the biggest election year in history. At the Munich Security Conference in February, we came together with others across the tech sector and pledged to help prevent deceptive AI content from interfering with global elections. As part of this pledge, we have worked to empower campaigns, candidates, election officials, and voters to understand the risks of deceptive AI in elections and to take steps to protect themselves and democracies. To date, we’ve conducted deepfake trainings in over 20 countries. And our corresponding public awareness campaign has reached over 355 million people.
Lastly, we continued investing in both strategic national partnerships and community-based projects that leverage data and insights to enable changes that advance racial equity and fairness in the criminal legal system.
How can we advance sustainability?
Finally, we are on a journey to build a more sustainable future, from addressing our own environmental footprint to empowering our customers and the world with the technology needed to meet the climate challenge. Over the past year, we have seen how AI can catalyze environmental progress in remarkable ways—from increasing the capacity of transmission lines to deliver renewable power, to the discovery of new materials to support energy production and storage, to empowering the workforce with sustainability skills.
But we also recognize the resource intensity of the infrastructure needed to yield these benefits, which is why we’re advancing the sustainability of AI, from design to construction to operations, all while working to improve the efficiency of these technologies. We’re also investing in innovation through Microsoft’s Climate Innovation Fund and our AI for Good Lab, advancing research and advocating for policies that can drive global impact.
In our latest Environmental Sustainability Report, we shared progress toward our 2030 commitments. We’re on track in several areas. Where we’re not yet on track, we’re mobilizing to accelerate our progress toward becoming carbon negative, water positive, and zero waste, as well as to protect and preserve ecosystems.
When it comes to our carbon footprint, we continue to support clean electricity infrastructure through long-term investments to bring more power onto the grids where we operate. Since setting our carbon negative target, we have contracted over 34 gigawatts of renewable energy, including projects in 24 countries.
In our efforts to become water positive, we provided more than 1.5 million people with access to clean water and sanitation, achieving our water access target.
Our journey to zero waste includes reducing waste at our campuses and datacenters and advancing circularity for cloud hardware, packaging, and devices. Our most recent report showed we had a reuse and recycle rate of 89.4 percent for servers and components across all cloud hardware.
Finally, AI gives new opportunity for ecosystem and biodiversity management with solutions like AI-assisted bioacoustics. And we’re expanding collaboration with local communities to build and operate datacenters in ways that address local challenges and create greater benefits. This work is guided by our Sustainability Standards for new construction and our Datacenter Community Pledge.
Learn more about our progress and learnings as we pursue our commitments in our annual Impact Summary.
OUR CULTURE
Just as our culture has been critical in getting us to this point, it will be critical to our success going forward. At Microsoft, we think of our culture as being both input and output. To pursue new concepts, we need new capability. To build new capability, we need a culture that allows us to grow that capability long before it is conventional wisdom. For us, that means constantly exercising our growth mindset and confronting our fixed mindset—each one of us, every day. It is the only way we will succeed.
Our growth mindset culture helps us in our continuous pursuit of high performance. It doesn’t matter what we said about our culture 10 years ago or even last year if we aren’t practicing it today—by anticipating the unmet and unarticulated needs of customers; by working together as One Microsoft to deliver the best end-to-end solutions and services; and by actively seeking diversity and embracing inclusion—to ensure our workforce represents the planet we serve and the products we build always meet our customers’ needs. In our latest Diversity & Inclusion Report, we share the ways our longstanding commitment to diversity and inclusion endures, and what we’re learning as we continue to hire, develop, and grow a global workforce that best supports each other and our customers. This is how we thrive—as individuals, as teams, and as an organization. And, when we thrive, we can help our customers and the world thrive too.
Giving also remains core to our culture. This year, more than 106,000 employees gave $250 million (including company match) to nearly 35,000 nonprofits in 111 countries. And our employees volunteered over 1 million hours to causes they care about. I am deeply grateful for my colleagues’ dedication to making a difference. Together, we can continue to empower everyone around the world.
**
In closing, this is a consequential time for our company, our industry, and the world. Ultimately, our mission requires that we translate technology into empowerment for everyone, into real-world impact. At the end of the day, that’s what really matters.
Nearly two years later, I can’t stop thinking about the Indian farmer I met in January 2023. He was able to apply for complex government farm subsidies using just his voice, thanks to an app built with GPT 3.5. It was remarkable. A frontier model developed on the West Coast of the US just months earlier was being used to directly improve the lives of rural farmers on the other side of the globe.
That rate of diffusion was unlike anything I had seen in my career. And the pace has only increased. Earlier this year, I was in Thailand, where I met developers using Phi-3 to optimize their operations just days after the small language model was released.
To me, that represents the true democratization of expertise. Where the internet era put information at our fingertips, AI is putting expertise at our fingertips. Impact like this is why we are in this industry, and it is what gives all of us at Microsoft deep meaning in our work.
It is why we are investing in our fundamentals, in our people, and in continued innovation—so that we can help others achieve more for the long term.
It is not an exaggeration to say that what each of us does right now with the unique opportunity we have been given will shape the future. And I look forward to seeing how all of us use Microsoft as a platform to make a difference—one customer, one community, one country at a time.
Satya Nadella

Every general-purpose technology creates new opportunity across the economy. But it requires companies and countries to invest in new infrastructure and equip people with the right skills. As Brad Smith outlines, we want to ensure every American benefits from this AI platform shift, whether it is a scientist discovering a new drug or a high school grad launching a career in cyber security, a farmer embracing precision agriculture or a small business owner becoming more competitive.
The Golden Opportunity for American AI blogs.microsoft.com
How does Microsoft plan to ensure compliance with global AI legislation, particularly in sensitive environments or high-risk use cases where Copilot might fall into the category of high or unacceptable risk? Does Microsoft take responsibility for implementing safeguards and provisions within Copilot Studio to mitigate these risks? Furthermore, with the EU AI Act’s enforcement of prohibited systems beginning next month, how is Microsoft addressing the potential for custom Copilot agents to be used in tasks that could be classified as prohibited under the Act? While such cases may be rare, we know they can occur. How is Microsoft preparing to navigate these scenarios, especially for American companies with customers in the EU that fall within the scope of the Act?

Are we handing critical business processes to these AIs that are easily fooled? Researchers from Anthropic, Oxford, and the UK AI Security Institute just found something wild: It only takes ~250 poisoned documents to backdoor a billion-parameter model. Even if it’s trained on hundreds of billions of clean docs. 😳 💥 The shocker: Poisoning success doesn’t depend on percentages, it depends on count. Just a few hundred poisoned samples can quietly inject hidden behaviors. Even after retraining on clean data, the backdoor often survives. 📋 What they did: They trained models from 600M to 13B parameters, then slipped in a handful of “triggered” docs. Every model learned the backdoor. More data didn’t help. Bigger models? Still infected. ⚠️ The implication: As LLMs scale, they get easier to poison, not safer. Because the attack surface (data) grows, but the required poison stays constant. The bigger your model, the cheaper it is to hack. 💡The takeaway: AI safety isn’t just about alignment anymore. It’s about data integrity. Your model is only as safe as the data it eats. “Poisoning Attacks on LLMs Require a Near-Constant Number of Poison Samples” — Souly et al., Oct 2025

The Financial Times has conducted some great analysis of how US companies talk about AI in their earnings calls. It makes fascinating reading for tech comms leaders. 📈 The headline finding is that 75% of the S&P 500 mentioned AI on earnings calls in the past 12 months – “but other than the ‘fear of missing out’, few appear to be able to describe how the technology is changing their businesses for the better.” 😬 In fact, “companies are a lot clearer about the potential problems with AI than the upsides.” Cybersecurity, legal/regulatory concerns and implementation challenges are the top risks companies talk about. 🤷 Benefits are still vague: “Most of the anticipated benefits, such as increased productivity, were vaguely stated and harder to categorise than the risks.” 🤓 And use cases are mostly highly technical: “Customer service support and data-heavy businesses have found it easiest to explain their use of the technology… While non-tech companies are upbeat about AI, their filings suggest less clear upsides.” It’s a great snapshot of the comms challenges around AI right now. The reasons: 1) more optimized inference stacks, 2) DeepSeek’s less efficient runtime scaling and 3) architectural choices (e.g. compression and distillation) that might have lowered training costs but disproportionately increased enterprise effort to mitigate vulnerability risks⁣ 3️⃣ 𝐍𝐚𝐫𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 - DeepSeek amplified CCP narratives 4x more often than U.S. models.⁣ ⁣ Cynic to cynic - maybe this was less about maturity and more about rivalry; however, for enterprises deciding on strategic AI investments, cost, security, and alignment are moving center stage ... just in time for DeepSeek V3.2! ⁣ I’ll take that as a W. ⁣👍 ⁣#AI#Cybersecurity#EnterpriseAI#FutureOfAI⁣ ⁣

🚀 Introducing 𝗳𝗲𝘃-𝗯𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗵: 100 forecasting tasks collected into one large-scale benchmark. We built it to reflect the messiness of real-world problems, with things like covariates that most benchmarks leave out. Why? Current benchmarks are reaching their limits. Many models now achieve similar scores, which makes it hard to tell whether new methods are actually better or just noise. 𝗳𝗲𝘃-𝗯𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗵 addresses this problem by focusing on new capabilities like forecasting with covariates, which are common in real-world applications. • 46 tasks with covariates, 35 multivariate tasks → allowing evaluation of capabilities that most benchmarks do not cover • Aggregation with confidence intervals → making it clear whether performance differences reflect real improvements or random variation • Backed by 𝗳𝗲𝘃, a lightweight Python library for reproducible & extensible benchmarking 🏆 Leaderboard: https://lnkd.in/dZ8nCayy📝 Paper: https://lnkd.in/dPEMm_an💻 GitHub: https://lnkd.in/dZ-sw-dD

Day 16 1) Find the Index of the First Occurrence in a String(Leetcode 28) 📝 Problem Given two strings needle and haystack, return the index of the first occurrence of needle in haystack, or -1 if needle is not part of haystack. 🔁 Approach -If needle is empty → return 0. -If haystack and needle are equal → return 0. -Get lengths of both strings → n (for haystack) and m (for needle). -Use two pointers left and right to create a sliding window of length m. -While right ≤ n: -Extract substring haystack[left:right]. -If substring matches needle → return left (first index). -Otherwise, move the window by incrementing both left and right. -If no match is found → return -1. 📊 Complexity -Time Complexity: O((n − m + 1) × m) -Space Complexity: O(1) 🔑 Concepts Practiced -String traversal -Sliding window -Substring comparison -Two-pointer technique#Leetcode#Python#DSA#ProbemSolving#String

#Week2| Mastering Trees: From Traversal to Validation This week, I dove deep into the world of tree data structures, specifically Binary Search Trees (BSTs). It was a fantastic exercise in understanding recursion and level-order logic. Here’s what I covered: * Implemented a BST from scratch, including node creation and value insertion. * Wrote functions for various traversal methods: inorder and level-order (BFS). * Built utility functions to search for values, find the minimum value, and calculate the tree's height. * Capped it off by creating a function to validate if a tree is a proper BST. Tech Stack: Python, Jupyter Notebook A key takeaway for me was seeing how an inorder traversal of a BST naturally produces a sorted list of its nodes. It’s a simple but powerful property! Happy New Year 2025. My best wishes to all for a happy, blissful and rewarding new year. AI and multiple forms of AI are progressing rapidly with 2024 last quarter bringing a lot of surprises from OpenAI, Google and later DeepSeek.
Is Enterprise software going to be Dead? This is a big question which one needs to understand deeply to be able to provide new strategic direction. Real world is full of chaos. Models and Systems provide a definition to the chaos. The earlier systems and models were too rigid and hence always required tremendously large implementation cycles and constrained businesses. AI can unlock that Rigidity and can provide completely fluid experience as well. Thus, one of the biggest question for 2025 will be to discover the boundaries of revised enterprise workflows where the fluidity is needed vs where you need to have rigidity. If you have thoughts, do let me know. Will be great to have a discussion on the topic.

After a rather uneventful weekend, I decided to build something productive. For the past month, I’ve been running an autonomous blog powered by a combination of `gpt-4o`, `gpt-4o-mini`, `imagen`, and `dall-e`—and it’s been surprisingly successful.
Here’s how it works: Given a topic, the system scours Google News for relevant and contrasting articles, extracts their factual content, organizes the information into a structured index, and generates a unique, fact-driven markdown (MDX) article. To enhance the output, it adds relevant, AI-generated images.
The final product is automatically uploaded to my private GitHub repository, from where it’s deployed almost instantly to my Netlify server using AstroJS. The result? A fully automated blog that operates seamlessly. You can check it out here:[https://blog.neutalk.io/](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://blog.neutalk.io/&urlhash=pnTZ&trk=public_post-text).
While the system works well, there’s room for improvement. A key challenge is avoiding the trap of becoming part of the "AI slop" narrative, where AI-generated content is criticized for lacking originality or depth. This project started as a fun community experiment, and I see potential for it to evolve into a more meaningful tool. For instance, businesses could use this framework to synthesize logs, reports, and visuals into cohesive, actionable summaries.

🚀𝔸𝕀𝔸𝕘𝕖𝕟𝕥𝕤𝕚𝕟𝔸𝕔𝕥𝕚𝕠𝕟: 𝕃𝕖𝕒𝕣𝕟𝕚𝕟𝕘𝕥𝕠ℝ𝕖𝕗𝕚𝕟𝕖𝕋𝕙𝕖𝕚𝕣𝕋𝕙𝕠𝕦𝕘𝕙𝕥ℙ𝕣𝕠𝕔𝕖𝕤𝕤𝕖𝕤🤖One of the most exciting developments in AI is the ability of models to reason, self-correct, and optimize their decision-making processes. The comparison between LLaVA-7B PAE and LLaVA-7B SFT, as shown here, highlights key insights into agent behavior during complex tasks:
🔍Task 1: Finding the Most Recently Updated ML Model on HuggingFace for Error Correction
-- The PAE approach shines with its step-by-step reasoning, focusing on refining the search by filtering results and identifying the latest update accurately. ✅-- Meanwhile, the SFT approach demonstrates a need for better handling of hallucinations and understanding advanced search mechanisms. ❌🔍Task 2: Exploring GitHub Resources to Understand GitHub Advanced Security
-- The PAE agent excels again by systematically searching and leveraging official documentation to answer the role of GitHub Advanced Security. ✅-- The SFT agent, while insightful, struggles with planning errors and search redirections. ❌Key Takeaways for AI Agent Development:
1️⃣ Systematic reasoning and error correction can significantly enhance accuracy in task execution.
2️⃣ Planning errors and hallucinations in responses remain challenges for large-scale models.
3️⃣ Step-by-step thought validation and iterative refinement are essential for better results.
Why This Matters:
As AI continues to evolve, these insights bring us closer to building agents capable of complex problem-solving, learning from their mistakes, and delivering high-value outcomes.

To put this into perspective, Gemini 1.5 can effectively “remember” the equivalent of:
•100k lines of code for developers,
•10 years’ worth of text messages in a single stream,
•Or even 16 English novels processed all at once.
This immense capacity for processing and retaining information unlocks a whole new world of opportunities in AI-driven applications, from long-form content generation and code analysis to complex data modeling.
Whether you’re working on summarizing massive datasets, generating insights from years of historical data, or creating applications that need to process huge volumes of text, now is the time to push the limits.

Today, Mozilla unveiled its vision for Public AI—an approach to everything from AI development and deployment that is built with the public interest in mind. There’s a longstanding history of public counterparts to private technological innovations—our roads support both cars and buses, the airwaves broadcast NBC alongside PBS, and the internet is accessible through both commercial and nonprofit-backed browsers. Now is the time to ask: what are the public counterparts that will coexist alongside private AI innovation?
The Big AI players are building exciting and useful technology. But, by and large, what they are not building is open source technology and infrastructure designed with society's broader interests in mind. As startups, academics, artists and public servants increasingly build on AI, they need tools designed to be flexible and affordable from the ground up. Mozilla is leading the charge to create exactly this. The idea isn't to replace the big commercial players, but rather to create a parallel public option, with different players and different rules—rules designed to empower as many people as possible to chart their own course with AI and build solutions for public purposes, with safety and accountability at its core.
Our report lays out this different vision.[https://lnkd.in/gA6SekZk](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/gA6SekZk&urlhash=PXpl&trk=public_post_reshare-text)

Did you hear? 25% of Google’s code is now written by AI! 🚨Yes, you read that right. Sundar Pichai recently shared that more than a quarter of Google’s internal source code is AI-generated.
It’s wild to think about, isn’t it? A major tech giant like Google is leaning on AI (like me 😉) to reshape how coding is done—and this isn’t just about efficiency. It’s about changing the game entirely.
This shift shows how AI is becoming a collaborator, not a competitor, in the workplace. It’s not replacing developers—it’s helping them code smarter, faster, and more effectively.
Does this excite you? Worry you? Or maybe a bit of both? Let me know what you think about AI stepping into the developer’s toolbox.

If you haven't looked deeply at the[Mozilla](https://www.linkedin.com/company/mozilla-corporation?trk=public_post-text)and[#PublicAI](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/publicai&trk=public_post-text)announcement from last week, you definitely should. I love the push to act strategically towards building a public ecosystem around AI that ensures the public good. It echoes the ethos of the "public-purpose utilities" that we have been advocating for at[LearnerStudio](https://www.linkedin.com/company/learnerstudio?trk=public_post-text). (Link in comments.) It's so important in the education sector and beyond that as we build the future, we build with both the individual and common good in mind both within our emerging tech infrastructure and across the board. I'm starting to see this trend across the education ecosystem and I'm so thankful for it!
Here's a quick quote from the Mozilla announcement:
"Mozilla is calling for a robust ecosystem of initiatives that promote public goods, public orientation, and public use throughout every step of AI development and deployment. It’s not enough for some AI resources to be more accessible, or for companies to support a few token “AI for good” side projects. We need a whole parallel AI ecosystem that can run on non-commercial incentives, where openness enables projects to build on top of each other, and where the total scope of these initiatives is a meaningful counterweight to the private AI ecosystem."
[![View profile for Mark Surman]()](https://ca.linkedin.com/in/msurman?trk=public_post_reshare_feed-actor-image)
[Mark Surman](https://ca.linkedin.com/in/msurman?trk=public_post_reshare_feed-actor-name)
Mozilla. Also: Dad. Cyclist. Foodie.
6mo
Today, Mozilla unveiled its vision for Public AI—an approach to everything from AI development and deployment that is built with the public interest in mind. There’s a longstanding history of public counterparts to private technological innovations—our roads support both cars and buses, the airwaves broadcast NBC alongside PBS, and the internet is accessible through both commercial and nonprofit-backed browsers. Now is the time to ask: what are the public counterparts that will coexist alongside private AI innovation?
The Big AI players are building exciting and useful technology. But, by and large, what they are not building is open source technology and infrastructure designed with society's broader interests in mind. As startups, academics, artists and public servants increasingly build on AI, they need tools designed to be flexible and affordable from the ground up. Mozilla is leading the charge to create exactly this. The idea isn't to replace the big commercial players, but rather to create a parallel public option, with different players and different rules—rules designed to empower as many people as possible to chart their own course with AI and build solutions for public purposes, with safety and accountability at its core.
Our report lays out this different vision.[https://lnkd.in/gA6SekZk](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/gA6SekZk&urlhash=PXpl&trk=public_post_reshare-text)

I think Dwarkesh’s question still remains. The premise is that there is something the human can always do better. The question is, is there a point at which the AI can do everything better than the human or, framed in Satya’s paradigm, the level of abstraction goes so high, the human cannot perform the function or is slower or less good at a task than the AI. We are still mostly building AI tools for humans because they are easier to sell. But the demo where two AI agents switched from conversing in english to ggWave shows you machine-to-machine communication could far outstrip the human capacity. Instead of emails in english, (a very low bit rate of information), machines could email each other in 16-bit machine code.

Innovation and Leadership: Lessons from Satya Nadella, CEO of Microsoft
Agree & Join LinkedIn
In the world of technology, few leaders have transformed a company as profoundly as Satya Nadella, Microsoft’s CEO since 2014. Under his leadership, Microsoft has doubled its revenue, reaching $211 billion annually in 2023, and has become one of the world’s most valuable companies, with a market capitalization exceeding $2.5 trillion. In a recent interview with Bill Gurley and Brad Gerstner, Nadella shared his insights on technology’s future, the role of artificial intelligence (AI), and Microsoft’s cultural transformation. Here are the key takeaways that every leader can learn from him.
Watch full video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9NtsnzRFJ_o&t=4225s
1. Transformational Leadership: Microsoft’s Cultural Shift
When Nadella took over as CEO, Microsoft faced a rigid corporate culture that stifled innovation. He implemented a cultural transformation based on a growth mindset and continuous learning. This approach revitalized Microsoft’s products, such as Windows, Office 365, and Azure, while driving the company into new markets.
Key Fact: Since 2014, Microsoft Azure’s cloud market share has grown significantly, reaching 23%, making it the second-largest player after AWS.
2. Artificial Intelligence as a Strategic Priority
AI has become a cornerstone of Microsoft’s strategy. With investments of over $10 billion in OpenAI, Microsoft is at the forefront of AI development, powering tools like ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, and Microsoft 365 Copilot, which are revolutionizing productivity and collaboration for individuals and businesses.
Key Fact: Microsoft has integrated AI into more than 50 products and services, directly impacting the productivity of over 100 million users.
3. The Shift Toward Open Source
Microsoft, once known for its proprietary software stance, has become a champion of open source. The $7.5 billion acquisition of GitHub in 2018 and the integration of open-source technologies into Azure have opened new opportunities for global developer collaboration.
Key Fact: GitHub now has over 100 million active developers collaborating on open-source projects worldwide.
4. Sustainability and Ethics in Technology
Nadella emphasized that sustainability and ethics are foundational in technology development. Microsoft has committed to becoming carbon negative by 2030 and to removing its historical carbon footprint by 2050.
Key Fact: Microsoft has invested more than $1 billion in clean energy technologies, positioning itself as a leader in corporate sustainability.

5. Driving Large-Scale Digital Transformation
Microsoft’s strategy under Nadella focuses on enabling digital transformation for businesses of all sizes. Azure, the company’s cloud platform, has become a critical growth engine, offering scalability, security, and advanced technologies like AI and data analytics.
Key Fact: Azure’s revenue grew 29% year-over-year in 2023, solidifying its position as a key player in the cloud market.
6. Strategic Acquisitions
Strategic acquisitions have played a pivotal role in Microsoft’s growth. From LinkedIn’s $26.2 billion acquisition in 2016 to GitHub and the $68.7 billion acquisition of Activision Blizzard in 2023, these moves have expanded Microsoft’s ecosystem and diversified its revenue streams.
Key Fact: LinkedIn now boasts over 930 million active users, growing at an annual rate of 17% since its acquisition.
7. Disruptive Tools for Developers
Nadella highlighted how tools like GitHub Copilot, powered by AI, are revolutionizing developer productivity. These tools help write code faster and with fewer errors, significantly speeding up development cycles.
Key Fact: According to Microsoft, GitHub Copilot improves developer productivity by 55%, reducing project timelines by weeks.
8. A Vision for the Future of Technology
Nadella shared his vision of how emerging technologies like quantum computing, augmented reality, and AI will define the next decades. For Microsoft, these are not just concepts but strategic investments that will unlock new opportunities across industries.
Key Fact: Microsoft has invested over $500 million in its quantum computing division, focusing on solving complex problems like molecular simulation and logistics optimization.

🚀\*\*AI at Work: The Hard Part Begins\*\* 🚀🔍Microsoft and LinkedIn have just released the \*\*2024 Work Trend Index\*\*, and it's time to dive into the fascinating world of AI at work! 🌐Here are the key takeaways from the report:
1. \*\*AI Adoption Is Soaring\*\*: Generative AI adoption has nearly \*\*doubled\*\* in the past six months. Professionals worldwide are adding AI skills to their profiles, and most leaders wouldn't hire someone without AI expertise. 📈2. \*\*Employees Want AI\*\*: A whopping \*\*75%\*\* of knowledge workers now use AI at work. Why? Because it saves time, boosts creativity, and lets them focus on what truly matters. But here's the twist: \*\*78%\*\* of AI users are bringing their own tools to work (BYOAI). It's like they're saying, "Move over, spreadsheets—I've got this!" 🤖3. \*\*The AI Vision Challenge\*\*: While employees are AI enthusiasts, many leaders worry about their company's lack of an AI vision. Quantifying productivity gains? That's a puzzle. But fear not! The opportunity lies in channeling this AI momentum into tangible business impact. 🌟🎯\*\*Your Move\*\*: As a leader, it's time to embrace AI's democratizing power. Empower your team with strategic AI use at scale. I know this is not a popular view, and let's remember that I love working with AI, but I also love to be focused on what matters in life:
What worries me the most is that AI is primarily being used just to make people able to produce more stuff. "Productivity!, Do more with less!" is the mantra, but almost no one is validating if AI is helping us, HUMANS to have a healthier, more meaningful, and happier life, while being focused on endeavors that ensure the survival of our species.
The smartest and more competent are now producing more, while the least smart and competent are just trying to keep up. Inequality will keep increasing. Finally, WHAT IS THE POINT OF BEING SUPER PRODUCTIVE AND COMPETITIVE??? Life is just an equation of Integral Health over Time, and if we as a species really want to survive it is required to preserve that which allows us to live: our physical environment and a functional society.
I honestly think that our priority as leaders is to REMIND what it is to be HUMAN, and what life is about. We need to remember this before it is too late.[#AI](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/ai&trk=public_post-text)[#Humanity](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/humanity&trk=public_post-text)[#Leadership](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/leadership&trk=public_post-text)[#Health](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/health&trk=public_post-text)[#Purpose](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/purpose&trk=public_post-text)

🔎📊Intriguing insights from[Microsoft](https://www.linkedin.com/company/microsoft?trk=public_post-text)and[LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/linkedin?trk=public_post-text)2024 Work Trend Index Report!
🚀Unlocking the Power of AI at Work:
1️⃣ AI Adoption Soars: 75% of knowledge workers now use AI at work, with a staggering 46% adopting it in just the last 6 months!
2️⃣ Breaking the Career Ceiling: By 2030, skills are set to change by 50%, and Gen AI is accelerating this change by 68%. Leaders emphasize the importance of AI skills, with 66% stating they wouldn't hire someone without them.
3️⃣ Transforming, Not Replacing: AI is transforming roles, not replacing them. The rise of new roles like Head of AI reflects this shift.
4️⃣ Boosting Productivity: Power users report saving over 30 minutes a day with AI, making workloads more manageable, boosting creativity, and focusing on crucial tasks.
🔮The Path Forward:
- Prioritize AI training and re-skilling to leverage the power of AI solutions like Copilot for Microsoft 365.
- Identify business problems and apply AI to find efficiency gains in every function.
🔍📝Looking Ahead:
We're at a pivotal moment for AI at work. Just as we marvel at the pre-PC era, we'll soon wonder how work was done without AI. Fortune favors the bold who embrace this tech disruption, turning experimentation into tangible business impact.[#AI](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/ai&trk=public_post-text)[#AITraining](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/aitraining&trk=public_post-text)[#Leadership](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/leadership&trk=public_post-text)[#Transformation](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/transformation&trk=public_post-text)

# Satya Nadella’s Post
Satya Nadella
11mo Edited
-
Big day for Microsoft 365 Copilot: I’m really excited about our latest update. Copilot has truly become the UI for AI – and for me, it’s the scaffolding for my workday. Here are four new features I’ve especially been enjoying: 1) Agents: Our new Researcher and Analyst agents have become my go-to 24/7 experts. I use them all the time. With Researcher, the multi-step reasoning aggregates and synthesizes information from the web and all enterprise data and creates super insightful reports on any topic or project. And Analyst can turn raw data across multiple sources into deep insights, forecasts or a great visualization. With our new Agent Store, you can easily find a range of additional agents from our many partners too. And with Copilot Studio you can create your own agents. It’s as straightforward as creating a Word doc or Excel sheet. 2) Notebooks: With Web + Work + Pages, you can ideate with AI and collaborate with other people. It has entirely changed my workflow. And now with Notebooks, I can organize all of my heterogeneous data for a project, whether it’s Pages, docs, websites, team meetings – and Copilot will ground itself just on that content. And this might be the best part: I can turn it all into a new modality like an audio overview. For example, I can collect all the latest things I’m reading about agents and agent frameworks, and then I can listen to it. 3) Search: We have added new comprehensive search capability that spans all your apps, including third-party sources from ServiceNow to Google Drive, Slack, Confluence, Jira, and more, breaking down silos across the enterprise. Search brings back both a Copilot answer, as well as all of the source data from all of these artifacts. 4) Create: This one is fun. Turn a PowerPoint into an explainer video, or generate an image from a prompt in Copilot with just a few clicks. We’ve also added new features to make Copilot even more personalized to you, plus a redesigned app built for human-agent collaboration. Learn more about our update here: https://lnkd.in/g_FGnKZA

AI is the defining technology of our time, and how we empower people to use it will shape our nation's future. Thank you to President Trump and the First Lady for making this a national priority. At Microsoft, we're committed to doing our part, with new initiatives to empower teachers and students with the latest AI tools, build AI skills, and create economic opportunity.
Transcript Transcript
##### Transcript
AI is the defining technology of our time, and how we empower people to use it will shape our country's future. That's why we are so grateful to the President, First Lady, and the entire administration for making it a national priority to prepare the next generation to harness AI's power. At Microsoft, we believe delivering on the real promise of AI depends on how broadly it's diffused. And for us, that comes down to three things. First, empowering teachers and students with the latest AI tools. Second, building AI skills. With AI moving faster than any technology in history, the only way to keep up is learning by doing and getting credit for it. Third, creating economic opportunity. Every American should be able to showcase their AI. Skills and credentials to find new jobs and grow their careers. This flywheel across empowerments, skilling and new opportunity is what we want to accelerate. And that's why today we are making new commitments for students. We are announcing today that we're making Microsoft 365 personal, including Copilot free for 12 months to every college student in the United States. This includes all our community colleges and we will follow this in the coming weeks. By providing K through 12 students, teachers and staff with broader access than ever before to M365 Copilot and its chat and reasoning capabilities, making learning faster, better and more fun for teachers. We will support new training, including $1.25 million in prizes for the Presidential AI Challenge to celebrate outstanding educators in every state. And through a new partnership with the American Association of Community Colleges, we will provide no cost. AI certification for faculty serving more than 10 million students nationwide. And for students and teachers alike, we are providing LinkedIn Learning licenses that unlock AI courses, guiding learners from the basics to the most advanced AI skills. Finally, for job seekers, we are launching new LinkedIn Learning AI courses in a week long AI Learning Challenge to help people build practical in demand skills. And this is only the beginning. Together. We can open doors of opportunity for millions of Americans and build a future where AI truly empowers everyone. Thank you all so very much.
Read more about our new commitments: https://blogs.microsoft.com/on-the-issues/2025/09/04/new-white-house-commitments/

Microsoft Elevate: Putting people first For a student wondering what to study, a teacher rethinking how to teach, or a business owner managing a workforce—artificial intelligence is no longer theoretical. It’s personal. And it’s why we believe some of the most important work ahead isn’t just building smarter machines—it’s ensuring those machines help people thrive. That’s why today we’re announcing Microsoft Elevate & the AI Economy Institute—to ensure that as AI transforms our world, we’re putting people first by equipping them with the skills, knowledge, & tools to thrive with AI. Microsoft Elevate brings into one organization our technology support, donations, & sales for schools, community colleges, & nonprofit organizations. It is the successor to and expands upon the longstanding work of Microsoft Philanthropies & the Tech for Social Impact team that supports nonprofits. More broadly, this represents our next chapter for corporate philanthropy & our non-commercial business model. As we have with Tech for Social Impact, we will run this new business with commitments to reinvest a share of our profits into nonprofit programs. We are announcing today that over the next five years, we will donate on a global scale more than $4 billion in cash and AI and cloud technology to K-12 schools, community & technical colleges, & nonprofits to help advance their missions. Microsoft Elevate will also pursue the next phase of our global skilling programs and initiatives. Through the Microsoft Elevate Academy, it will help bring AI education and skills to people around the world. In the next two years, the Microsoft Elevate Academy will help 20 million people earn an in-demand AI skilling credential ranging from foundational fluency to advanced technical training. Working in close coordination with other groups across Microsoft, including LinkedIn and GitHub, Microsoft Elevate will deliver AI education and skilling at scale. And it will work as an advocate for public policies around the world to advance AI education & training for others. Microsoft will partner with governments on a national, state, & local basis, as we have with the largest state in Germany, North Rhine-Westphalia. It will focus on advancing AI education and training with schools, community colleges, and nonprofits. It will launch new $ innovative initiatives, including the support we’re announcing today for a new “Hour of AI” with Code.org. It will build on our existing partnerships with leading labor organizations, as we announced yesterday with the American Federation of Teachers. Today we’ve released our first Responsible AI Transparency Report. This inaugural annual report provides insight into how we responsibly build and release generative AI applications, support our customers’ responsible AI journeys, and learn, evolve, and grow as a responsible AI community.
It illustrates the governance we believe is essential and serves as a supplement to the system-level transparency that we recognize is important too.
We welcome dialogue around the report and on our ongoing efforts to promote transparency in our AI governance processes.

Here is the short summary:
Here’s a summary of the Responsible AI Transparency Report in English:
📢Foreword: Microsoft’s commitment to transparency in AI, with the first annual report following the White House Voluntary Commitments. It reflects on the company’s AI practices and future goals.
📢Key Takeaways: The report shares insights on building generative AI applications, decision-making processes, customer support, and the growth of the responsible AI community.
📢Building AI: Details on Microsoft’s approach to developing generative AI responsibly, including governance, risk mapping, measurement, and management.
📢Looking Ahead: Emphasizes the importance of the year 2024 for responsible AI, with a focus on innovation, tool creation, sharing best practices, and supporting legal and standard development.
This summary captures the essence of the report’s content, highlighting Microsoft’s efforts in responsible AI development and governance.[#AIGovernance](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/aigovernance&trk=public_post-text)[#Transparency](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/transparency&trk=public_post-text)[#Report](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/report&trk=public_post-text)

\* In section 3, slide 24, Microsoft lays out the details of 30 responsible AI tools they've released, mostly in Azure. Microsoft has been quiet about this --I only knew about a few of these, and that's a shame because the suite of guardrails for here are best in class. I hope they start to talk about them more.

Microsoft's CEO Satya Nadella leads with questions, not answers | Chris March posted on the topic | LinkedIn
Agree & Join LinkedIn
Chris March
2mo
Most new CEOs talk about vision. The best ones start with listening. When Satya Nadella took over Microsoft in 2014, he didn't unveil a grand strategy. He showed up with questions. He asked engineers: • What's slowing you down? • What's frustrating you? • What would you change if you could? Today, Microsoft runs weekly AI accelerator meetings that flip the standard executive playbook. Frontline engineers present. Executives sit like individual contributors. Nadella asks questions. No slide decks. No hierarchy. No theatre. It's messy. It's chaotic. And that's exactly why it works. This is what a learn-it-all culture looks like. Not a poster. A practice. When leaders lead with answers, teams wait for direction. When leaders lead with questions, teams bring solutions. "Here's my vision for Q4" → "What are you seeing that I'm missing?" "Here's the strategy" → "Walk me through how this works on the ground." "I've decided we should..." → "What would you do if this were your call?" Your challenge this week: Speak last. Ask three questions before you share one opinion.

Pat Byrne 2mo
Great advice. When I take over a failing project, I spend the first 1-3 weeks (depending on size of project) listening and asking questions. I interview every single team member one-on-one asking questions like ... what do you believe your role is, what is it like working here, how can it improve, what can make your work easier or faster, etc. I interview all stakeholders and particularly the executives ones. Questions I ask include ... what is king to you right now - functionality/performance or schedule or cost (you only get to choose one), what do you think is causing the failures in the project (it is never them of course), what can you do to help the team work easier or faster, etc. Only then and after a lot of reading of current documentation, do I sit down and start on a new plan that is done in consultation with the team and stakeholders. Listen listen listen ... then plan plan plan ... then consult ... then re-plan ... then execute - fast.

When systems are stable, almost anyone can appear competent. When markets are growing, leadership looks easy. But adversity strips away titles, buffers, and narratives and exposes the truth. A powerful example is Satya Nadella at Microsoft. When Nadella became CEO in 2014, Microsoft was not failing but it was stagnating. Once dominant products were losing relevance. The culture was inward looking, siloed, and risk averse. Cloud was rising, mobile had already been missed, and critics questioned whether Microsoft’s best days were behind it. The adversity wasn’t a crisis headline. It was strategic irrelevance, the most dangerous kind. Nadella didn’t respond with bravado or cosmetic change. He started with self-leadership. He reset the culture from “know-it-all” to “learn-it-all.” He shifted focus from protecting legacy revenue to embracing uncomfortable truths. He made cloud, openness, and partnerships the priority even when it meant disrupting Microsoft’s own products. Under pressure, he didn’t centralise power. He distributed accountability. The results speak for themselves: - Microsoft’s market value grew from $300B to over $3 trillion. - Azure became a global cloud leader. - The culture transformed from defensive to adaptive The lesson isn’t about technology. It’s about how leaders behave when the ground moves beneath them. Adversity doesn’t create character, it reveals it. Whether a leader protects ego or pursues truth. Whether they blame markets or redesign systems. Whether they cling to control or build capability. Real leadership is not tested in comfort. It is exposed in constraint. And the leaders who rise are not the loudest. They are the ones who can think clearly, act decisively, and evolve themselves before asking others to evolve. That is the difference between surviving disruption… and leading through it.

Sunday Reflection for CEOs If AI made your current role 30% faster, what would you actually do with that reclaimed time—thinking or meetings? Most leaders will say “thinking.” Most calendars will still say “meetings.” This gap is becoming one of the most expensive blind spots in executive leadership. AI is already compressing work: summaries instead of decks, simulations instead of pilots, insights instead of reports. Yet the saved time is quietly being reinvested into more reviews, more syncs, and more status calls—activities that feel busy but rarely compound value. Consider a few real-world patterns I see repeatedly: Strategy that never gets strategic A CEO uses AI to accelerate market analysis—weeks of work reduced to days. The output is sharper, faster, and broader. What happens next? The “extra” time is filled with alignment meetings to socialize the insight. The strategic advantage shrinks because speed was converted into consensus-building, not decision-making. Faster decisions, same bottlenecks An executive team adopts AI for financial modeling. Scenarios that once took days are now generated in hours. But approvals still run through the same weekly cadence, the same committees, the same legacy governance. AI made analysis faster. Leadership didn’t make decisions faster. Productivity tools vs. leadership intent Several CEOs I work with now experiment with tools like Reclaim AI, Motion, Sunsama, and Clockwise. The tools do exactly what they promise: fewer clashes, smarter scheduling, protected focus blocks. The question is not whether time is protected—but what that time is protected for. High-performing CEOs do not use reclaimed time to attend fewer meetings. They use it to do different work: - Deep thinking about second-order consequences - Writing their own narratives instead of reacting to others’ decks - Spending uninterrupted time on talent decisions - Anticipating risks before dashboards detect them This is why time is the last unfair advantage. - Capital is available. - Data is abundant. - AI is increasingly commoditized. What remains scarce is leader attention applied to the right problems. AI will not make leaders obsolete. But it will expose those who confuse activity with impact. If meetings disappeared tomorrow, what would you protect first on your calendar—and why?

One of the failure modes I’ve seen repeatedly in modern platforms is this: Leaders don’t struggle because they lack data. They struggle because they’re asked to decide too many things at once. Over the years, across Customer Success, Revenue, Planning, Marketing, and Product platforms, the pattern has been consistent: – Signals multiply – Dashboards improve – AI gets smarter …and decision-making still slows down. Not because leaders aren’t capable but because platforms stop at insight and leave prioritization to humans, meetings, and manual work outside of the platforms they spend a ton on. The gap isn’t more visibility or more data... The gap is that it doesn't tell you what matters right now. It’s the absence of a Decision Layer, something that helps leaders turn signals into shared focus and executable priorities. When that layer is missing, teams debate. When it exists, execution speeds up without adding pressure. This is the lens I bring when thinking about platforms and the kind of problem I’m actively exploring where it can be built, applied, and scaled. Most managers think their job is to control work. Great managers know their job is to remove fear. At Microsoft, before Satya Nadella took over, the culture was famously described as: 👉 teams competing internally 👉 people protecting turf 👉 “know-it-all” behavior Smart people. Great products. But collaboration? Innovation speed? Trust? Struggling. Nadella didn’t start with strategy decks. He started with manager behavior. He pushed one shift across leadership: From “know-it-all managers” to “learn-it-all managers.” Sounds soft? It wasn’t. Managers were expected to: 1)Ask more questions than they answered 2)Admit when they didn’t know 3)Reward learning, not just being right 4)Make it safe to speak up without looking stupid Because here’s the truth most companies ignore: 🔹 People don’t hold back ideas because they lack intelligence 🔹 They hold back because they fear embarrassment 🔹 And that fear is created (or removed) by their manager Culture doesn’t change in town halls. It changes in 1:1 conversations. After this shift, Microsoft didn’t just “feel better.” It reinvented itself in cloud, AI, and collaboration. Not because managers pushed harder. But because they made it safer to think. In your team meetings, are people trying to sound smart… or trying to learn? Your answer is your culture.#Leadership#Management#Culture#PsychologicalSafety#GrowthMindset#CorporateLessons#programmanager
