Delivering 3 trusted platforms for the AI age
Agree & Join LinkedIn
The following is adapted from my remarks at Microsoft Ignite this morning.
With every platform shift, it is good to understand the context and underlying forces. And today it's scaling laws. With Moore’s law, we saw a doubling in performance every 18 months. With AI, we have started to see performance double roughly every six months.
There is a lot of debate about whether we've hit the wall with scaling laws. At the end of the day, these aren’t physical laws but empirical observations that hold true over long periods of time. So it's valuable to debate and have that motivation for innovation in model and system architectures as well as data regimes. In that context, if anything, we are seeing the emergence of a new scaling law with test-time compute, which OpenAI's o1 and Copilot's "Think Deeper" feature leverage to solve harder problems.
Ultimately, these breakthroughs manifest in three capabilities:
The first is a new universal interface that is multimodal. It supports speech, images, and videos both as input and output. Second, we have new reasoning and planning capabilities. We essentially have a new "neural" algebra to approach complex problems. We can detect patterns and make predictions involving people, places, and things, as well as interactions between them. And third, we now have the capability to support long-term memory, rich context, and teach these models how to use tools.
When we put all of this together, we can build a rich agentic world defined by a tapestry of AI agents that can act on our behalf across work and life, across teams, business processes, and organizations.
We are going to celebrate a lot of technology over the next multiple days, but it’s also worth reflecting on the words of philosopher John Haugeland. "The trouble with artificial intelligence,” he said, “is that computers don’t give a damn.”
But we do!
And that’s really what grounds us. Amidst all this rapid change, we remain focused on our mission to empower every person and every organization on the planet to achieve more.
Today I want to focus on AI and business transformation, and how it drives growth and improves efficiency and operating leverage.
To that end, we are building three platforms: Copilot, Copilot devices, and the Copilot and AI stack.
### Security
Before I dive in, I want to talk about something we are prioritizing above all else: Security.
At this conference, you will hear us talk a lot about our Secure Future Initiative, as we implement our principles of secure by design, secure by default, and secure operations. Most importantly, we are committed to continuous improvement, because our work is never done. After all, we are only as good as our ability to defend against the next novel attack.
At the end of the day, we recognize that security is a team sport. It’s why we partner broadly with the security community, and why today we announced Zero Day Quest., a new hacking event with $4 million in rewards focused on securing cloud and AI—the highest of any public hacking event in the industry. The quest starts today and will culminate in an in-person hacking event next year.
### Copilot
Now, let’s talk about Copilot. Copilot is the UI for AI.
It‘s rapidly becoming an organizing layer for work and how work gets done. Every employee will have a Copilot that knows them and their work, helping them unlock productivity, enhance creativity, and save time.
Copilot Studio will allow you to create agents that automate business processes. And every IT department will have a control system to manage, secure, and measure impact.
Over the past year, we have seen incredible momentum. What “lean” did for manufacturing, AI will do for knowledge work. It’s all about increasing value and reducing waste.
And we’re just getting started. We are continuously innovating, shipping hundreds of updates since we made Copilot generally available a year ago. That includes real work on fundamentals like quality. Copilot responses are now more than two times faster on average, and response satisfaction has improved nearly three-fold.
Going forward, we think about Copilot adoption in three ways: The first is adoption. The more employees use Copilot and Copilot agents, the faster they realize value. The second aspect is extensibility. Copilot is a platform you can extend with agents that scale what you can do. And the third is measuring ROI.
Let’s start with Copilot Pages. Pages is the first artifact of the AI age.
I use Pages myself to prep for customer meetings. Copilot can comb through everything about the customer from the web, LinkedIn, biz apps, and work artifacts like emails, documents, and presentations. I can put all of that into a Page and share it, including with the account team. And then it becomes the first draft of meeting notes that are shared with everyone involved in real-time.
Copilot is also deeply integrated across Microsoft 365. In Teams, Copilot can reason over past meeting chats and transcripts to get you quickly up to speed. With Teams screen understanding, Copilot can even answer questions about presentations and documents shared during meetings.
In Word, Copilot creates a draft based on other Word documents, PowerPoint decks, PDFs, emails, and meetings. You never have to start with a blank page.
In PowerPoint, you enter a prompt about what you want your presentation to be about, and Narrative Builder generates an outline. You can edit and refine its suggestions and create a first draft of the complete presentation.
And in Outlook, prioritize my inbox is a total game changer. It helps you quickly get to the messages that matter, analyzing your inbox based on both the content of the email and the role of the sender.
And I think what we are doing in Excel is fantastic. Take a look:
Now, let’s talk about how to extend Copilot. Today, I was very excited to announce Copilot Actions. With Actions, you can use Copilot to reduce the amount of repetitive, everyday tasks that you do. It is like Outlook rules for the AI era. Actions are a simple but powerful way for you to scale the work you do, and they work across the entire Microsoft 365 system.
We also introduced new agents you can use with your team. You can think of them as teammates scoped to specific roles and permissions. You can add a Facilitator Agent to your Teams meeting to help you stay focused, moderate discussions, and follow up on action items; our Project Manager Agent in Planner helps automate key steps in the project management workflow; and our Employee Self-Service Agent provides useful information and answers to policy questions at your company.
We are also announcing SharePoint agents. Every SharePoint site now has a built-in agent. These agents provide instant access to real-time information and insights from your knowledge base in the flow of your work.
We are also giving you the ability to easily create your own agents with Copilot Studio. Sometimes we mystify AI and agentic creation. But it’s as simple as creating a Word doc, PowerPoint slide, or an Excel spreadsheet.
You can also make these agents autonomous using Copilot Studio. And they can always raise an exception in Copilot for input. After all, at the end of the day, even autonomous agents need a UI to interface with us.
And of course, when we talk about extensibility, that includes changing how you interact with your bespoke business applications, too. We are excited to share that many of our partners have built their own agents and connectors in Microsoft 365 Copilot. This includes Adobe, LinkedIn, SAP, ServiceNow, Workday, and even new AI-first agents built by companies like Cohere.
Here it is in action:
Now, let’s talk about measurement. After you adopt Copilot and extend it with agents, you need to measure the ROI. And to do that, today we introduced Copilot Analytics.
Our goal is to show how Copilot usage is directly translating to business outcomes across Sales, Marketing, Finance, and more.
## Copilot devices
This AI era will be defined not just by what’s happening in the cloud, but also what’s happening at the edge.
Over the past year, we have introduced an entirely new class of Windows PCs designed to unleash the power of distributed AI across cloud and edge. We call these Copilot+ PCs.
We are working across our entire ecosystem—including AMD, Intel, and Qualcomm, along with every major OEM—to bring these to life, with silicon capable of an incredible 40-plus TOPs. When it comes to fundamentals, they are the fastest PCs on the market and include all-day battery life.
We are also delivering entirely new endpoints. Three years ago, we introduced the Cloud PC category with Windows 365, which securely streams your personal Windows desktop from the cloud to any device, whether it runs iOS, Android, or is a mixed reality headset like the Meta Quest.
We have seen great momentum and adoption as we bring Cloud PCs to remote and temporary workers, IT, developers, frontline employees, and for disaster recovery. In fact, it is my go-to dev desktop, equipped with full access to GitHub, VSCode, Azure SDK, CLIs—all set up to use on any of my devices.
Today, I am excited to announce the next step with Windows 365 Link:
It’s a simple, secure, purpose-built device for Windows 365. It is admin-less and password-less. And security configurations are enabled by default and cannot be turned off.
Form factors like Windows 365 Link give you yet another choice for secure Windows endpoints. We recognize how mission-critical Windows is, and we are committed to security and resiliency as first-class priorities. And today we announced the Windows Resiliency Initiative, as we double down on our commitment to make Windows secure and reliable for customers and their mission-critical workloads. As part of this work, we are making changes to OS access, introducing new features, and, in partnership with our ecosystem, establishing new guidelines for Safe Deployment Practices.
### Copilot & AI stack
We are also exposing every layer of our tech stack so you can build your own copilots and agents.
### Infrastructure
At the infrastructure layer, we are building Azure as the world’s computer. Over the past year, we have made datacenter investments in 15 countries across six continents. We now have 60-plus datacenter regions—more than any other provider.
And we are not stopping there. Today, we went further, announcing Azure Local.
Enabled by Azure Arc, Azure Local extends Azure services across hybrid, multi-cloud, and edge locations, with a central control plane. It brings Azure services to customers' distributed locations, whether in retail, hospitality, or industrial manufacturing, so they can run mission-critical workloads across cloud and edge.
When it comes to silicon, our Cobalt 100 VMs became generally available last month. Customers and partners like Databricks, Siemens, and Snowflake are seeing up to 50 percent improvements in price-performance with these VMs. Our own media processing capabilities in Teams now run on Cobalt 100 as well.
Our approach to silicon includes a deep commitment to security, and today we announced our first new in-house security chip, Azure Integrated HSM.
It is a dedicated hardware security module that hardens key management, meaning encryption and key signing can remain within the bounds of the device without compromising performance or security. Starting next year, it will be part of every new server deployed on Azure, enhancing security for both confidential and general-purpose VMs and containers.
Our goal is to remove any bottlenecks that stand in the way—whether it is performance, latency, or resource constraints. That is why today we are expanding Azure Boost with our first in-house DPU.
DPUs are processors specifically architected to accelerate data-centric workloads, absorbing multiple components of a traditional server into a single piece of silicon. It runs cloud storage workloads at three times less power and four times the performance.
When it comes to AI, we are building new intelligence factories for the world. Tokens per watt plus dollars is the new currency for performance. It is all about maximizing value in the most efficient way.
The pace of innovation across the industry is phenomenal, and we are working with our partners to bring you more choice across cost and performance.
Today, we announced the preview of NVIDIA Blackwell AI infrastructure on Azure. With 72 Blackwell GPUs in a single NVLink Domain and InfiniBand in the backend, these racks are optimized for the most cutting-edge and high-scale AI workloads.
We are also working closely with AMD. We were the first cloud to offer VMs powered by AMD’s MI300X GPU, and we are using this infrastructure to power Azure OpenAI Service, too.
And today, we are introducing Azure HBv5, co-engineered with AMD. It is up to eight times faster than any other cloud VM, setting a new standard for high-performance computing. It will be generally available next year.
### Data
Now, let’s move to data. There is no AI without data. For you to build your AI apps, you need to rendezvous your data with your AI compute effectively. We are building out the full data estate to do just that.
At the core of our platform is Microsoft Fabric. It brings together all your data, as well as your analytics workloads, into one unified experience. With OneLake in Fabric, it is easy to unify your data no matter where it lives—in Azure, on premises, or even in AWS or GCP—to create the data layer for your AI workloads.
We are very excited to announce that we are bringing our flagship operational database, SQL Server, natively to Fabric, with Microsoft Fabric Databases.
Fabric simplified every aspect of an organization’s analytics needs. We want to do the same for operational databases. With Microsoft Fabric, customers now have an enterprise data platform that serves the full range of use cases—from batch data to real-time, to massive transactional performance—all in a unified product.
### Azure AI Foundry
Now, let’s talk about building your AI applications. Every new generation of apps has brought a changing set of needs. Just like web, mobile, and cloud led to new app platforms, AI is transforming how we design, customize, and manage apps today.
That is why we are building a first-class app server for the AI age. We call it Azure AI Foundry.
With Foundry, we are unifying all our models, tooling, safety, and monitoring solutions into a single experience integrated with the world’s most popular developer tools, available as a standalone SDK and as a portal.
It all starts with our approach to models. We know models and model choice sit at the core of every use case. We want to help you choose the right model for the right job. In fact, we now have over 1,800 models in our catalog.
OpenAI continues to set the pace for model innovation, and all of their latest models are available on Azure. We also support open-source models from Meta and Mistral, as well as providers like Cohere. New categories of models are also emerging. In fact, we have added over 20 industry models designed for more specialized use cases from partners like Bayer, Paige, Rockwell, Siemens, Sight Machine, and others.
With all this model diversity, it has never been more important to have the right tools to choose models for the job at hand. With Foundry, we are adding model experimentation capabilities, meaning for the first time you will be able to experiment with several of these models to compare outcomes and choose the best model that works for you.
We’re also excited to introduce a new service designed to simplify the creation of AI-powered agents with Foundry. Our new Agent Service helps developers build, deploy, and scale AI apps that automate business processes.
While anyone can go to Copilot Studio to build agents with just a few clicks, developers who need more control can go directly to the Agent Service and build agents code-first, grounded with data wherever it lives—be it public data from the web, enterprise data in SharePoint, or leveraging Fabric’s OneLake to unify your data estate across your apps and cloud providers. These agents can take action across any system using our 1,200-plus Logic Apps connectors.
We also know that the multi-agent space is evolving quickly, so we are ensuring our early multi-agent frameworks can all be used within Foundry Agent Service.
AI apps have specific operational considerations, too, and organizations also need a centralized way to manage AI costs, performance, safety, and security. That is why I was excited to share that we are also bringing new management capabilities to Foundry. For example, we are introducing AI Reports, which developers can use to document and share their application's use case and evaluation results.
Safety is the most important feature of any AI application. We continue to ensure we have the best tools to build safe and secure AI apps, including Prompt Shields that detect and block the manipulation of outputs that could harm your business's content.
Here's AI Foundry in action:
## Close
I’ll end where I started. It all comes back to our mission: to empower every person and every organization on the planet to achieve more, one community, one country at a time.
As we enter this new age of AI, it is up to us to empower human achievement. That is why, over the past year, we have helped train and certify over 23 million people in AI and digital skills. And we are committed to working to help millions more. Because learning these skills can change people’s lives. In fact, it already has.

(That is what expanding TAM + good execution looks like) A few other highlights from across the stack: 1/ Cloud & Token Factory: The key metric we are optimizing for is tokens per watt and per dollar, which is all about increasing utilization and decreasing TCO using silicon, systems, and software. A new app platform is born. You can think of agents as the new Apps. Fabric is also gaining momentum, now at a $2B annual run rate. You can read more about our results here: https://lnkd.in/g92aPQj9
Q-results aren’t just financials anymore - they’re signals about where the monopoly power of platforms will ebb or flow in an AI-powered world. Instead of pure revenue beats, what stands out is this: the most valuable companies are recalibrating the core levers of productivity and trust simultaneously. Cloud growth is no longer separate from AI adoption; it’s defined by it. And that means every enterprise that still treats AI as a feature instead of the operating system of modern business is already behind. My controversial view: At scale, incumbents aren’t being disrupted by startups pushing better products - they’re being outpaced by ecosystems that turn AI from a tool into a default economic substrate. The next decade won’t be about better software. It will be about who owns, orchestrates, and governs the AI-driven workflows that actually run the enterprise. If the winners of the next decade will be those who network AI into every value chain, what does competitive advantage look like in a world where AI is both commodity and strategic moat?

With an AI business run rate of $13 billion and 175% year over year growth the focus is shifting from simple adoption to long term efficiency. A key observation is the pivot to tokens per watt. This is a significant change in the infrastructure playbook. It suggests that the next decade of cloud growth will be defined by unit economics and the rigorous optimization of silicon and software. As agents become the primary application platform the industry faces a new challenge. We are moving from monitoring static code to observing complex intent. The ability to provide clarity within these non deterministic systems is becoming a strategic necessity. The $40.9 billion in cloud revenue is impressive but the underlying shift in how we build and observe these platforms is the real story for 2026.

Just reported our quarterly results. We are still in the beginning phases of AI diffusion and its broad GDP impact, and already we’ve built an AI business that is larger than some of our biggest franchises that took decades to build. Our quarterly cloud revenue crossed $50 billion for the first time. What’s striking is it was less than 10 years ago that our annual cloud revenue was $10 billion! (That is what expanding TAM + good execution looks like) A few other highlights from across the stack: 1/ Cloud & Token Factory: The key metric we are optimizing for is tokens per watt and per dollar, which is all about increasing utilization and decreasing TCO using silicon, systems, and software. A good example is the 50% increase in throughput we were able to achieve in one of our highest volume workloads – OpenAI inferencing powering our Copilots. We will also continue to scale our new 1P systems like Maia 200 with every gen, and look for the best economics and innovation from our ecosystem partners. 2/ Agent Platform: Like in every platform shift, all software is being rewritten. A new app platform is born. You can think of agents as the new Apps. We’re providing everything customers need to build their own agents, including model catalogs, harnesses for orchestration, context engineering, plus AI safety and observability. Already, over 1.5K customers have used both Anthropic and OpenAI models on Foundry. 250+ customers are on track to process >1T tokens on Foundry this year. Fabric is also gaining momentum, now at a $2B annual run rate. And we’re creating a new category w/Agent 365, which extends governance, identity, security, and management to agents. 3/ High Value Agentic Experiences: We are entering an age of macro delegation and micro steering across domains. Intelligence using multiple models is built into multiple form factors. Lots of momentum across our family of Copilots: • Consumer: Daily users of Copilot app up nearly 3X year-over-year • Productivity: 15M paid M365 Copilot seats, and multiples more enterprise Chat users • Coding: 4.7M paid GitHub Copilot subs, up 75% year-over-year • Healthcare: 21M patient encounters documented, up over 3X year over year 4/ Bottomline: Our TAM will grow substantially across every layer of the tech stack as this diffusion accelerates and spreads, and we feel very good about how we’re delivering for our customers today and innovating to capture the opportunity ahead. You can read more about our results here: https://lnkd.in/g92aPQj9

Meanwhile, real-world deployments: • Chemical plant agents → $𝟭𝟰𝗠 𝗮𝗻𝗻𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗰𝘁 • Steel mill process intelligence → 𝟯𝟭% 𝗱𝗼𝘄𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 Billions invested in infrastructure. Just reported our quarterly results. We are still in the beginning phases of AI diffusion and its broad GDP impact, and already we’ve built an AI business that is larger than some of our biggest franchises that took decades to build. Our quarterly cloud revenue crossed $50 billion for the first time. What’s striking is it was less than 10 years ago that our annual cloud revenue was $10 billion! (That is what expanding TAM + good execution looks like) A few other highlights from across the stack: 1/ Cloud & Token Factory: The key metric we are optimizing for is tokens per watt and per dollar, which is all about increasing utilization and decreasing TCO using silicon, systems, and software. A good example is the 50% increase in throughput we were able to achieve in one of our highest volume workloads – OpenAI inferencing powering our Copilots. We will also continue to scale our new 1P systems like Maia 200 with every gen, and look for the best economics and innovation from our ecosystem partners. 2/ Agent Platform: Like in every platform shift, all software is being rewritten. A new app platform is born. You can think of agents as the new Apps. We’re providing everything customers need to build their own agents, including model catalogs, harnesses for orchestration, context engineering, plus AI safety and observability. Already, over 1.5K customers have used both Anthropic and OpenAI models on Foundry. 250+ customers are on track to process >1T tokens on Foundry this year. Fabric is also gaining momentum, now at a $2B annual run rate. And we’re creating a new category w/Agent 365, which extends governance, identity, security, and management to agents. 3/ High Value Agentic Experiences: We are entering an age of macro delegation and micro steering across domains. Intelligence using multiple models is built into multiple form factors. Lots of momentum across our family of Copilots: • Consumer: Daily users of Copilot app up nearly 3X year-over-year • Productivity: 15M paid M365 Copilot seats, and multiples more enterprise Chat users • Coding: 4.7M paid GitHub Copilot subs, up 75% year-over-year • Healthcare: 21M patient encounters documented, up over 3X year over year 4/ Bottomline: Our TAM will grow substantially across every layer of the tech stack as this diffusion accelerates and spreads, and we feel very good about how we’re delivering for our customers today and innovating to capture the opportunity ahead. Microsoft just announced Q2 Earnings so here’s a quick post about the numbers we posted. M365 Copilot - It was a record quarter for M365 Copilot, officially crossing 15 million paid M365 Copilot seats and growing more than 160% year over year. - M365 Copilot is quickly becoming part of the daily workflow, with daily active users up 10x year over year. - The number of customers deploying Copilot at real scale is rising fast: customers purchasing more than 35,000 seats tripled year over year, including Fiserv, ING, NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration, the University of Kentucky, The University of Manchester, Westpac. This is what makes Microsoft such a remarkable place to work right now. AI isn’t a slide, a side project, or an experiment. Yes, we’re early. Yes, we’re learning. And yes, this transformation will be uneven. But calling AI a bubble misses the point entirely. A good example is the 50% increase in throughput we were able to achieve in one of our highest volume workloads – OpenAI inferencing powering our Copilots. We will also continue to scale our new 1P systems like Maia 200 with every gen, and look for the best economics and innovation from our ecosystem partners. 2/ Agent Platform: Like in every platform shift, all software is being rewritten. A new app platform is born. You can think of agents as the new Apps. We’re providing everything customers need to build their own agents, including model catalogs, harnesses for orchestration, context engineering, plus AI safety and observability. Already, over 1.5K customers have used both Anthropic and OpenAI models on Foundry. 250+ customers are on track to process >1T tokens on Foundry this year. Fabric is also gaining momentum, now at a $2B annual run rate. And we’re creating a new category w/Agent 365, which extends governance, identity, security, and management to agents. 3/ High Value Agentic Experiences: We are entering an age of macro delegation and micro steering across domains. Intelligence using multiple models is built into multiple form factors. Lots of momentum across our family of Copilots: • Consumer: Daily users of Copilot app up nearly 3X year-over-year • Productivity: 15M paid M365 Copilot seats, and multiples more enterprise Chat users • Coding: 4.7M paid GitHub Copilot subs, up 75% year-over-year • Healthcare: 21M patient encounters documented, up over 3X year over year 4/ Bottomline: Our TAM will grow substantially across every layer of the tech stack as this diffusion accelerates and spreads, and we feel very good about how we’re delivering for our customers today and innovating to capture the opportunity ahead. You can read more about our results here: https://lnkd.in/g92aPQj9

Today, at Microsoft Build we showed you how we are building the open agentic web. It is reshaping every layer of the stack, and our goal is simple: help every dev build apps and agents that empower people and orgs everywhere. Here are 5 big things we announced today: 1. Coding agent: We are taking GitHub Copilot from being a pair programmer to peer programmer. You now have a full coding agent built right into GitHub. You can assign it issues – whether it’s bug fixes, new features, or ongoing code maintenance. And it will complete these tasks autonomously. 2. Copilot tuning: Copilot can now learn your company’s unique tone and language. It is all about taking that expertise you have as a firm and further amplifying it so everyone has access. 3. Agent factory: Foundry is the complete app platform for building apps and agents. We are adding support for more models from Grok, Hugging Face, Meta, Mistral, and more. Plus: Agentic retrieval in Azure AI Search, Foundry Agent Service, integration with Copilot Studio, and more. And we are ensuring the tools you already use for identity, management, and security will now all extend to agents too. 4. NL Web: This is a new open project that lets you use natural language to interact with any website. Think of it like HTML for the agentic web. 5. Microsoft Discovery: We’re bringing together the full tech stack to help speed up science itself. Discovery uses agents to generate ideas, simulate results, and learn. A great example is this promising candidate for a coolant that doesn’t rely on forever chemicals. You can read more about all of this – and much more – here: https://lnkd.in/gik2mTNb

OpenAI just launched the Atlas browser, and it feels like the first real architecture shift in how we use the web in over a decade. Chrome, Safari, and Edge all basically render pages, manage tabs, expose APIs for extensions. Arc tried to evolve that by turning the browser into a workspace, wrapping Chromium in better UI and context management. Atlas embeds ChatGPT directly into the browsing pipeline. Instead of a request–response model, it introduces a reasoning layer on top of the DOM. The assistant can parse content, interpret structure, and take multi-step actions across sites using persistent context. It's not just injecting summaries into the UI but rather treating the web as a programmable environment. Browsers will behave more like runtime agents than interfaces. The page becomes a *data source* not a destination. This changes how developers should think about the web stack. Sites built for Atlas-style environments will need machine-readable semantics, structured metadata, and clear permission layers for automation. The browser is parsing meaning and chaining tasks across origins. If this model takes off, front-end frameworks, analytics, and even SEO logic will have to evolve around agent compatibility.

On this day in 1994, Netscape Navigator launched. It wasn’t just a software release; it was the start of the modern internet. As Fortune’s Beatrice Nolan and Jeremy Kahn wrote this week, “The browser wars are back.” But the battlefield has changed. Andreessen Horowitz's Marc Andreessen gave the world a way to see the web. Until then, the internet was mostly command lines and text interfaces. Netscape turned it into something visual, interactive, and intuitive. Suddenly, the world wasn’t typing to machines, it was navigating them. Fast forward 31 years, and that same idea — how humans interact with information — is being reinvented again. Only this time, the browser isn’t the window we look through. It’s becoming the agent that looks out for us. It’s not Chrome vs. Safari or Edge vs. Firefox anymore. It’s Google vs. Perplexity. ⭕ Opera vs. ֎ OpenAI. And the new competition isn’t about tabs, extensions, or speed; it’s about intelligence, context, and trust. AI-powered browsers like Perplexity’s Comet and Opera’s Neon aren’t just tools to take us to a page. They’re becoming assistants that act on those pages; reading, summarizing, booking, even emailing - all on our behalf. As George Chalhoub, assistant professor at UCL Interaction Centre (UCLIC), put it, for 30 years, browsers were about “type, click, explore.” Now, it’s shifting to “ask, delegate, done.” It’s not just browsing anymore. It’s delegating. Think about that for a second. For decades, we learned to navigate the web. Now, the web, powered by AI, is learning to navigate for us. It’s poetic, really. Andreessen's (cofounder of Netscape) original vision made the internet accessible. Today’s AI agents are making it actionable. And that brings us full circle to Andreessen’s enduring influence. He didn’t stop at building the first great browser. He built a new playbook for how startups shape culture, from Netscape to a16z, from browsers to blockchains to AI. He’s still marking the map for what comes next, only now, instead of helping us find information, his investments help founders build the intelligence that finds for us. When you zoom out, it’s clear: The next platform war isn’t about who owns the search bar; it’s about who owns the interface between human intention and machine execution. Netscape helped us explore. AI browsers are helping the internet explore us.#AI#Internet#Fortune#a16z Photo Credit: Jon Erlichman Fortune Article: https://lnkd.in/e_Ku-7-B

A Positive-Sum Future
Agree & Join LinkedIn
I’ve been thinking a lot about what the net benefit of the AI platform wave is. The real question is how to empower every company out there to get more out of this platform shift and build their own AI native capabilities and enterprise value (vs inadvertently just transfer their unique value to the tech sector!!).
Bill famously said a platform is when the economic value of everybody that uses it exceeds the value of the company that creates it. That’s the essence of the positive-sum future.
Even in our somewhat zero-sum mindset industry, we can create partnerships that create value for all parties involved. Our partnership with OpenAI is a great example. Our investment helped them scale; their research accelerated our own innovation. That’s what healthy platforms and partners do—they catalyze and compound progress.
There’s no better proof than what we announced just this week. The world’s first AI superfactory was co-designed with OpenAI and informed by three generations of AI supercomputers we built for frontier model training and inference. It was also a result of working closely with Nvidia and getting better at the full stack optimization from model architecture to micro-architecture of the chip and everything between three companies!
We also did the work to bring AMD into the fleet doing inference of GPT models, which enabled them to get up to speed on their own software stack for AI.
And now all this infrastructure will scale to support every startup to enterprise doing their own training to inference.
You can see the same dynamic in coding. Thanks to AI, the category itself has expanded and may ultimately become one of the largest software categories. I don’t ever recall any analyst ever asking me about how much revenue Visual Studio makes! But now everyone is excited about AI coding tools. This is another aspect of positive sum, when the category itself is redefined and the pie becomes 10x what it was! With GitHub Copilot we compete for our share and with GitHub and Agent HQ we also provide a platform for others.
Of course, the real test of this era won’t be when another tech company breaks a valuation record. It will be when the overall economy and society themselves reach new heights.
When a pharma company uses AI in silico to bring a new therapy to market in one year instead of twelve. When a manufacturer uses AI to redesign a supply chain overnight. When a teacher personalizes lessons for every student. When a farmer predicts and prevents crop failure. That’s when we’ll know the system is working.
Let us move beyond zero-sum thinking and the winner-take-all hype and focus instead on building broad capabilities that harness the power of this technology to achieve local success in each firm, which then leads to broad economic growth and societal benefits. And every firm needs to make sure they have control of their own destiny and sovereignty vs just a press release with a Tech/AI company or worse leak all their value through what may seem like a partnership, except it's extractive in terms of value exchange in the long run.
We know that the Internet wave had tremendous positive sum impact in the world, and yet we also had some sectors that got hollowed out like local media. This time around we have the opportunity to ensure broad diffusion of this tech with choice and control that is distributed to ensure positive sum outcomes across the board.
At the end of the day, this new technological wave gives us the opportunity to dream bigger and set higher ambitions for what we can collectively achieve. Each of us will need to play our part!

# Digital Transformation
[Satya Nadella](https://www.linkedin.com/in/satyanadella)![Satya Nadella]()
### Satya Nadella
#### Chairman and CEO at Microsoft
Published Apr 4, 2016
[+ Follow](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=/pulse/digital-transformation-satya-nadella&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_publisher-author-card)
This past year, I’ve been inspired by our customers, partners, government leaders and people driving change in their communities around the world. Each of them are eager to renew growth and make a greater impact on society, but they’re faced with the realities of today’s markets and constraints. To move forward, businesses of all sizes, in every industry, are pursuing transformation with digital technology.
This week, at[Envision](http://news.microsoft.com/envision2016/?utm_campaign=Envision+2016&utm_medium=bitly&utm_source=News+Center#sm.00001lt63417a4csuv2h93sreh50a), we’re sharing our perspective of where technology is headed —its impact on society at large and on business specifically. Much of that view is centered on asking and answering two key questions:How is your business being changed by digital technology?And how is your core business model being changed by digital technology?
Irrespective of their industry, organizations today must start thinking and operating like digital companies.
This transformation isn’t just about procuring a CRM, ERP, or office automation system.It requires building out what we refer to as systems of intelligence —digital feedback loops that help you better engage with your customers, empower your employees, optimize your operations, and reinvent products and business models. These systems of intelligence encompass your people, processes and technology.And they will ultimately define your competitiveness and ability to change the landscape of the industries you participate in.
Today, Microsoft announced partnerships in two industries ripe for transformation:automotive and financial services.
With Toyota, we’re[partnering in a new venture called Toyota Connected](https://blogs.microsoft.com/transform/2016/04/04/toyota-and-microsoft-connect-drivers-to-the-future/#sm.00013oc4w6k0fdrdxs21bstpqbv22)that will introduce intelligent services designed to make driving more intuitive, predictive and safe.
With R3, we’re[enabling blockchain as a service in Azure](http://news.microsoft.com/2016/04/04/microsoft-and-r3-partnership-to-accelerate-adoption-of-distributed-ledger-technologies-by-global-banks/#sm.00001lt63417a4csuv2h93sreh50a), making it possible for over 40 of the top financial institutions in the world to clear transactions and transfer assets instantaneously and directly.
Each of us are part of our economies and our societies.Long-term growth is directly related to our ability to make our climate more sustainable, our economies more viable and our societies more equitable.Those changes can only happen if we ask ourselves two questions:What difference is our business making? And what difference are we making?
At Microsoft, we’re proud to partner with and empower all our customers around the world with the leading technology to seize the vast opportunities ahead.

The Sales Agent’s ability to 𝐚𝐮𝐭𝐨𝐧𝐨𝐦𝐨𝐮𝐬𝐥𝐲 𝐪𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐟𝐲 𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐬 and execute low-impact sales hinges on advanced NLP combined with RL from historical CRM interactions. It likely employs a multi-task learning framework, optimizing for lead scoring, intent detection, and personalized outreach generation. The system’s capacity to operate 24/7 stems from its asynchronous processing architecture, where it continuously ingests updates from CRM triggers and external data feeds, refining its decision-making via a feedback loop tied to sales outcomes. A big leap over static workflows, as it dynamically adapts to shifting customer behaviors and market conditions. On the other hand, Sales Chat's strength lies in a RAG mechanism, where embeddings of CRM records, pitch decks, and meeting transcripts are indexed and queried in real time. When a prompt like “list deals at risk” is issued, it performs a vector-based similarity search across these embeddings with 𝐭𝐞𝐦𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐚𝐥 𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐥𝐲𝐬𝐢𝐬 𝐨𝐟 𝐝𝐞𝐚𝐥 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐦𝐞𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐜𝐬 from the CRM. The result is a concise, prioritized output that reduces the cognitive load on sales reps, allowing them to focus on strategic engagement rather than data aggregation.

Imagination Vs Memory Paper Beyond Memory: Why Human Imagination Is Not Retrieval? And Why Current AI Does Not Truly Imagine? Abstract: Human imagination allows the generation of novel mental constructs without prior sensory reference. Current AI systems, however advanced, operate by recombining past experiences or datasets, a process fundamentally distinct from true imagination. This conceptual paper highlights the difference between memory-based reconstruction and reference-free imagination, and outlines the implications for understanding human cognition and developing artificial intelligence systems that aspire to emulate genuine imaginative capacities. Introduction: Imagination is a cornerstone of human cognition, enabling creativity, planning, and problem-solving. Despite the claims of AI researchers, most artificial intelligence models do not truly imagine; they operate by processing and recombining existing data. This paper seeks to delineate the conceptual boundaries between human imagination and memory-based processes, proposing a clearer understanding of what genuine imagination entails.

What’s interesting here isn’t just task automation — it’s the shift in how work itself is structured. For decades, software required humans to orchestrate every step: analyze → decide → execute. Agent workflows start to invert that model. Humans define the intent and constraints, and the system coordinates the execution. The real leadership challenge won’t be learning new tools. It will be learning how to design environments where autonomous systems can operate safely and productively. I explore this idea in my book series “Operating in the Dark” (available on Amazon and Apple Books): in complex systems, outcomes rarely come from direct control — they emerge from how the system is structured. Curious how you see this evolving: Will the most valuable skill soon be task execution, or system design?

Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella's full interview with CNBC
CNBC's Jon Fortt sat down with Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella in advance of the company's annual Build conference.
The tech company reported higher than expected third-quarter earnings last month with strong guidance for the upcoming fiscal year.
Microsoft plans to continue driving revenue growth through its cloud services. Its Intelligent Cloud segment contains the Azure public cloud, which competes with Amazon Web Services.
Nadella discussed the future of Microsoft and its cloud service, rivals Google and Amazon, artificial intelligence, and what to expect at this year's Build.
Read the full transcript here.
Jon Fortt: Great. I think it's been seven years or so since we first sat down.
Satya Nadella: That's right. And I guess that was before I was CEO. That's right.
Fortt: Yes, before you were CEO. We go way back, you know.
Nadella: That's right.
Fortt: And you're famous now.
Nadella: What happened?
Fortt: You wrote a book. I think I saw you in Better Homes and Gardens even. [EDITOR'S NOTE: Nadella appeared in Good Housekeeping, not Better Homes and Gardens.]
Nadella: That's right.
Fortt: I've never seen, that I can recall, an executive go from "Who's he?" to now, "This guy knows what's going on, and he is at the center of the zeitgeist," quite the way I've seen you do it. Is it weird?
Nadella: If you say so. I don't find any difference, but it's been a fantastic ride. I think what's been humbling for me is — I don't get confused — this is all because of the platform I've been given, which is Microsoft and the work we do. It's not me, but it's the opportunity I've been given.
Fortt: At what point do you say, and I know this isn't a turnaround, this is hitting refresh, and there's still work to do — as you said in the book — but Microsoft's stock is near all-time highs, in the mid 90s, recently. At what point can you say, "Yeah, we've got that part behind us. The turnaround, or the refresh, or whatever you want to call it, there's still more to do, but yeah, good job, team. Now it's time to take the next hill."
Nadella: I mean, I think that's the fundamental posture that we have to have. And quite frankly, any company that needs to continue to renew itself needs to have. Because if you think about it, what's going to happen next, and the day after, is what you have to anticipate. So what has happened in the past is just the past.
Fortt: Is Phase One done? Can you kind of say, "Hey, I've been CEO for four-ish years now, and yeah, we got that part done, pretty good job."
Nadella: Yeah I think to me it is very important to start with two things — a very clear sense of purpose and identity, and a clear understanding of how to express that sense of purpose and identity in a changing world. I feel like in the the last four and a half years, we were able to do a good job of that.

say 70 years ago, there have been two real dreams we’ve had. The first is can computers
understand us instead of us having to understand computers? And second, in a world where we
have these this ever increasing of people, places and things, right, and so as you digitize more
artifacts on peoples, places and things, and you have more information, can computers help us
reason, plan and act more effectively on all that information?
Those are the two dreams that we’ve had for the last 70-plus years, and here we are. I think that
we’ve had real breakthroughs on both fronts. The core underlying force is one of the questions I
always ask myself, which is like, "OK, this is great, and this is maybe like the golden age of
systems, but what’s really driving it?"
I always come back to these scaling laws, and so just like Moore’s Law helped to drive the
information revolution, the scaling laws of DNNs are really – along with the model architecture
and the interesting ways to use data and generate data, it’s really driving this intelligence
revolution. You could say Moore’s Law was probably more stable in the sense that it was scaling
at maybe 15 months, 18 months. We now have these things that are scaling every six months or
doubling every six months.
Effectively, what we have, though, with these scaling laws is a new natural user interface that’s
multi-modal. That means supports tech, speech, images and videos, and it has input and output.

across our apps and devices. We have new reasoning and planning capabilities that help us

But before we get into that, I'm seeing these headlines about the toll of the earthquake in Turkey and Syria, the death toll topping 7,000. Microsoft is a global company. What's your outlook on that and the response?
Yeah, I mean, first of all, our heart goes out to everyone impacted, and in fact our first priority was to ensure even the safety of our own employees in Turkey and Syria. And we've activated our disaster response so that we can be there for any institution, any organization that we can help.
And we've also activated our philanthropies in giving, so that we can all support the people of Turkey and Syria as they go through this. In the Pacific Northwest, this is something that we think a lot about. And so really our heart, and we'll spare no effort in helping.
Absolutely. And I do want to turn to your news of the day, which is pretty monumental in technology. Today's announcement, affecting search, affecting browsing. You said it's a new day for search, the race starts today. Google rules search and browsing. Best case, how long is it going to take you to dethrone them?
Well, first of all, it's, to me, these paradigm shifts or platform shifts are a great opportunity for us to innovate. The first thing that is a priority for me is not about dethroning anybody. It's more a priority for us to say, well, how can we rethink what search was meant to be in the first place? In fact, Google's success in the initial days came by reimagining what can be done in search. And I think the AI era that we're entering gets us to think about it. And that's what we’re really motivated by, Jon, and then what you saw today in terms of really building not just a new search experience but thinking of it as what's a copilot for the web look like in this AI era is what's exciting.
What we saw is a large language model helping to not just compose queries but deliver detailed answers about things like a multi-day travel itinerary. Information about artists in Mexico. Pretty deep. People have been playing with ChatGTP and DALL-E for a while. You're bringing this to market now. What's the economic opportunity?
Well, I mean, we already have an at-scale search business even though our share, as you rightly pointed out, is very, very small. The good news is we start with already a business that is profitable. And here's the interesting thing: the most profitable, large software business is search. So I look at t...

But you can see why[**Satya Nadella**](https://news.microsoft.com/exec/satya-nadella/)might be the exception. He has worked at Microsoft for more than 30 years, nearly 10 as C.E.O. At the start of the personal-computer era, Bill Gates’s Microsoft was a behemoth, eager to win every competition and crush every rival. But the internet era put the company on its heels; newer firms like Google, Facebook, and Amazon were more nimble, more innovative — and maybe hungrier. Jeff Bezos of Amazon would[reportedly refer to Microsoft](https://www.nytimes.com/2015/08/16/technology/inside-amazon-wrestling-big-ideas-in-a-bruising-workplace.html)as “a country club.”
But under Nadella, Microsoft has come roaring back. He invested heavily in what turned out to be big growth areas, like cloud computing. Microsoft has always been in the business of acquiring other companies —[more than 250](https://www.crunchbase.com/search/acquisitions/field/organizations/num_acquisitions/microsoft)over its history — but some of the biggest acquisitions have been Nadella’s: LinkedIn, Nuance Communications and, if regulators allow, the gaming firm[Activision Blizzard](https://www.reuters.com/markets/deals/potential-outcomes-ftc-legal-bids-block-microsoft-activision-deal-2023-06-16/). And there have been many more key acquisitions, like GitHub, where computer programmers store and share their code. The central thesis and goal we have with AI starts with our mission, to empower every person and every organization on the planet to achieve more.

That’s what the cloud enables. Even the cloud is not a single destination but a distributed fabric. That’s what’s driving all of these ambitions that we have in terms of technology.

Satya Nadella New Zealand Visit 2026
# Satya Nadella’s New Zealand Visit 2026
Microsoft Chairman and CEO, Satya Nadella, visited New Zealand on April 21, 2026. Watch his keynote from the Microsoft AI Tour and catch the latest news, photos and videos from his visit here.
## Watch the keynote
## Pairing geotechnical data with AI helps New Zealand to build better
After the tragic Christchurch earthquake, New Zealand built a geotechnical database to help engineers get complex information on the ground beneath its buildings. Now that data can be retrieved and queried in natural language via Beca’s digital twin platform BEYON and its AI assistant, powered by Microsoft Azure and Azure OpenAI in Microsoft Foundry. By combining AI with underground data, engineers can now make faster, better decisions on how New Zealand builds, and rebuilds, above ground.
## Microsoft doubles its commitment to AI and digital upskilling in New Zealand
Microsoft has announced it will double its existing AI and digital skilling commitment in New Zealand, opening access for a further 200,000 people to skilling by the end of 2028. This will help build an AI-ready workforce and supports the goals of New Zealand’s national AI Strategy to accelerate AI adoption and build capability.
## Charting Aotearoa’s digital horizon: a new chapter in innovation, partnership, and growth
Jane Livesey shares the findings of a new Economic and Social Impact report for New Zealand, capturing how in FY25, Microsoft contributed a total of NZ$9.4 billion to New Zealand’s economy. From datacentre investments to supporting customer productivity and partner growth, the findings demonstrate Microsoft’s significant contribution to New Zealand’s prosperity.

[...]
Quote of the day by Satya Nadella
[...]
Meetings are a part of everyday life at work these days. Teams get together to talk about ideas, check on progress, and make decisions that affect people and projects. But in a lot of these talks, only a few people speak up while others stay quiet. Satya Nadella's quote, “The next time you are in a meeting, ask the quietest person what they think. Invite everyone into the conversation. If you are on a conference call, ask the people on the phone to share their thoughts first”, really stands out here. Let everyone join in the conversation. If you're on a conference call, ask the people on the other end to say what they think first.The message is clear and strong. It shows how important it is to be included, listen, and take part in a group. When working together is important for success, making sure everyone is heard is no longer just a good habit. [...]
During a wide-ranging discussion at the World Economic Forum 2026, Nadella said that while the competition with fellow tech giants like Google, Meta and other is fiercer than ever, the expanding role of technology in the global economy makes this a “blessed” time for the industry. His comments came amid a “pretty intense” era led by artificial intelligence (AI) and shifting global markets.“It's a pretty intense time. I mean, so the way I always think is, it's always helpful. When you have a complete new set of competitors every decade, because that keeps you fit,” Nadella said. Reflecting on his 34-year journey at Microsoft, Nadella dismissed the idea that the AI race is a zero-sum game, instead pointing to a future where tech’s share of the world's GDP will only continue to rise.

The coming year will mark a decisive phase for artificial intelligence, as the technology moves from early experimentation to widespread, real-world deployment, Microsoft chief executive Satya Nadella said in a note on Tuesday.Reflecting on the year ahead, Nadella argued that AI is transitioning from the “initial phase of discovery” to “widespread diffusion”, with companies and societies beginning to separate spectacle from substance. While progress in model capabilities has been rapid, he cautioned that the industry is still “in the opening miles of a marathon”, with much of AI’s ultimate impact yet to be determined.The Microsoft CEO said one of the most urgent challenges is redefining how humans relate to AI. Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella defined a true AI platform as one where user value surpasses the creator's. He emphasized empowering businesses with AI-native capabilities, citing Microsoft's "positive-sum" partnership with OpenAI as a model. Nadella stressed that the AI era's success will be measured by real-world industry gains, not just tech valuations.
[...]
Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella recently shared a post explaining what he believes defines a real artificial intelligence (AI) platform — and he turned to a well-known idea from Bill Gates to make his point. In a detailed post on X (formerly Twitter), Nadella said tech companies should focus on building AI systems that create more value for the people and businesses using them, not just for the companies that make the technology. He cited Bill Gates to emphasize the same: “A platform is when the economic value of everybody that uses it exceeds the value of the company that creates it.”Tesla CEO Elon Musk responded to Nadella's post with a facepalm emoji.
[...]
Nadella said this idea matters even more during the current AI boom, where many firms risk giving away too much of their own value to big tech platforms. “The real question is how to empower every company out there to build their own AI-native capabilities,” he wrote.

# Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella on 'another big step' in company’s AI infrastructure, shares photo: 'We are the first cloud company to...'
[...]
Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella has announced that his company has become the first cloud provider in the world to install Nvidia Vera Rubin NVL72 system for validation. This is seen as a significant milestone in the global race to build the next generation of artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang unveiled the Vera Rubin NVL72 AI supercomputer at CES, with the company promising up to 5x greater inference performance and 10x lower cost per token than Blackwell – its top-tier AI chip currently available in the market.Nadella shared the news alongside a photo of the system. “We’re the first cloud to bring up an NVIDIA Vera Rubin NVL72 system for validation, another big step in building the next generation of AI infrastructure with NVIDIA,” he said.

A conversation with Satya Nadella, Chief Executive Officer of Microsoft.
Speakers: Satya Nadella, Laurence D. Fink
The 56th Annual Meeting of the World Economic Forum will provide a crucial space to focus on the fundamental principles driving trust, including transparency, consistency and accountability.
This Annual Meeting will welcome over 100 governments, all major international organizations, 1000 Forum’s Partners, as well as civil society leaders, experts, youth representatives, social entrepreneurs, and news outlets.
The World Economic Forum is the International Organization for Public-Private Cooperation. The Forum engages the foremost political, business, cultural and other leaders of society to shape global, regional and industry agendas. We believe that progress happens by bringing together people from all walks of life who have the drive and the influence to make positive change.

- Language: en
## Description
Microsoft’s CEO Satya Nadella joined me for a conversation at Build 2025 to dive deep into how AI is transforming the developer experience, and the future of work.
We talked about the growing role of Copilot in writing code (it now contributes to over 30% of Microsoft’s own codebase), and what the introduction of Copilot Tuning means for developers and companies who want to train AI on their own proprietary data. Satya shared his vision for “AI squads” specialized agents that assist with tasks like email, data analysis, and ideation, and how Microsoft Teams is enabling these multi-agent systems to collaborate seamlessly.
TIMESTAMPS
We also explored critical questions around trust, transparency, and security in AI systems. Satya detailed how Microsoft is building safety directly into its AI engineering pipeline—from agent identity management and audit logs, to new tools like Prompt Shields and Microsoft Peer Review that help ensure responsible deployment of AI across the enterprise.
One of the most powerful stories he shared was from Stanford Medicine, where multi-agent systems are being used to enhance tumor board meetings, integrating pathology, clinical trial data, and medical literature to improve decision-making in cancer care.
Finally, I asked Satya how he hopes to look back on his legacy at Microsoft. His answer centered not on individual success, but on maintaining a culture of innovation and impact—one that stays mission-driven, no matter how much the technology changes.
This conversation offers an inside look at the tools, principles, and ideas driving Microsoft’s vision for AI—and what it means for developers, businesses, and society at large.
If you're building with AI, thinking about its impact on your work, or just curious about where we're headed—this one is worth watching.

Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella and BlackRock Chairman & CEO Larry Fink discussed the future of AI, productivity, jobs, and global economic growth at the World Economic Forum in Davos. From AI agents and software automation to fears of job disruption and inequality, the exchange highlighted how AI could reshape economies—if diffusion is done right.

Amazing. And by the way, I

## Description
While he hardly needs an introduction, few leaders have shaped the future of technology quite like Satya Nadella.
He stepped into Microsoft’s top job at a catalytic moment—making bold bets on the cloud, embedding AI into the fabric of computing, all while staying true to Microsoft’s vision of becoming a “software factory.”
In this conversation with Aditya Agarwal and Ruchi Sanghvi, Satya discusses:
• his approach to building early at Microsoft and why we all need to continue “refounding”
• the future of AI and why critical thinking will remain at a premium
• Microsoft’s partnership with OpenAI, his vision for quantum, and more

## Description
As part of this interview, Satya Nadella gave me and Dylan Patel (founder of SemiAnalysis) an exclusive first-look at their brand-new Fairwater 2 datacenter.
Microsoft is building multiple Fairwaters, each of which has hundreds of thousands of GB200s & GB300s. Between all these interconnected buildings, they’ll have over 2 GW of total capacity. Satya then answered a bunch of questions about how Microsoft is preparing for AGI across all layers of the stack.
